components.meta_desc_en: Browse PC components: processors, motherboards, RAM, storage, graphics cards, power supplies, cases, cooling and cables for builds and upgrades.
components.seo_desc_en: Internal parts and supporting hardware used to build, upgrade or repair desktop computers, workstations and servers. The category covers processors, motherboards, memory, drives, graphics cards, power supplies, cooling, cases and the cabling that connects them. Subcategories are organized by part type so a system builder can plan a balanced configuration without searching across vendors.
components.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

This section gathers the internal hardware that goes inside desktops, workstations and servers, along with the cabling, cooling and protection devices that keep a build running. The structure follows the way a system builder thinks: pick each part once, in order, and verify it fits the rest of the configuration before adding it to the order.

What this category covers

Processors, motherboards, memory, storage drives, graphics cards, power supplies, cases, fans and cooling, plus connectivity items like data and power cables, hubs, KVM switches, and protection devices such as UPS systems and Power Distribution Units (PDUs).

Who this category is for

System integrators, IT departments handling fleet upgrades, repair shops, and technical buyers specifying parts for new builds or replacements. The catalog assumes the buyer already knows the platform and form factor they are working with.

How to choose components

Start with the platform

Most decisions follow from the CPU socket and motherboard chipset. Once those are fixed, the memory type, storage interfaces, and the form factor of the case and power supply are constrained.

Match power and cooling to the load

Power supply wattage and cooling capacity should be sized for the components you intend to run, with margin for upgrades. Undersizing either is a common cause of crashes, throttling and shortened component life.

Plan storage in tiers

Working data lives on fast NVMe SSDs; bulk and archive data on SATA SSDs or HDDs. Decide the split before buying so the motherboard has the right number of M.2 and SATA connections, and the case has enough drive bays.

Think about the next upgrade

Picking a motherboard with spare DIMM slots, a case with extra drive bays, and a power supply with headroom usually costs little upfront and avoids a full rebuild a year or two later.

What to check before buying

  • Will this part fit the existing motherboard, case and power supply, or will those need to be replaced too?
  • Is the component compatible with the operating system and any existing software stack?
  • Are the right cables and connectors included, or do they need to be ordered separately?
  • Is the working environment dusty, hot, or limited in airflow? Cooling and filter requirements change with the setting.
  • Does the build need redundancy (dual PSU, RAID, ECC memory) for the workload it serves?

Frequently asked questions

How do I know which parts are compatible with my motherboard?

Start with the CPU socket and chipset listed on the motherboard. From there the memory type (for example DDR4 or DDR5), drive interfaces (M.2 NVMe, SATA), expansion slots and form factor are all constrained. The board manufacturer publishes a Qualified Vendor List (QVL) that confirms tested combinations, especially for memory.

What is the difference between consumer and server components?

Server-class parts prioritize uptime, error correction and 24/7 operation: ECC memory, redundant power supplies, hot-swap drive bays and longer firmware support. Consumer parts focus on peak performance and price. Mixing them is possible but the gain is limited; a server build benefits most from a server-class motherboard and PSU.

Can I reuse components from an older build?

Storage drives, GPUs, peripherals and sometimes power supplies travel between builds without issue. Memory and CPUs depend on the platform and rarely move forward more than one or two generations. Always check whether the old part still meets the performance the new system needs.

How do I size a power supply for a new build?

Add the rated power of the CPU and GPU, then add roughly 30 percent for drives, fans, motherboard and headroom. Choose a unit with the right efficiency rating: 80 PLUS Bronze or higher for office systems, Gold or Platinum for systems that run constantly. A modular cable design helps keep airflow clean.

Do I need a dedicated graphics card?

Only if the workload requires it. Office and basic creative work runs well on integrated graphics in modern CPUs. Discrete cards are needed for 3D rendering, computer-aided design, video editing, machine learning, gaming, and driving multiple high-resolution displays.

What protection do I need beyond the components themselves?

An Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) protects against short outages, voltage sags and spikes; a Power Distribution Unit (PDU) organizes power feeds in a rack. For systems that run continuously, both are usually worth specifying alongside the build, not as an afterthought.

components/cables.meta_desc_en: Find data and power cables, USB and interface hubs, and card readers for desktops, notebooks and peripheral connections.
components/cables.seo_desc_en: Connecting hardware for PCs, notebooks and peripherals: data cables, power cables, USB and interface hubs, plus card readers and adapters. The group brings together items typically bought alongside a build or as replacements when an existing cable fails. Subcategories separate signal cables from power feeds, and from the hubs that expand a single port into several.
components/cables.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Cables and small connectivity hardware are the connective tissue of a working setup. This section groups the parts that move data and power between a PC, its peripherals and any expansion equipment, along with the hubs and adapters that bridge mismatched ports.

What this category covers

Data cables for displays, storage and networking; power cables and extension leads; USB and interface hubs; and card readers used to transfer files from memory cards. Subcategories break these out by purpose so the right item is easy to find.

Who this category is for

IT teams stocking spare cables for fleet repairs, system builders finishing a new desktop, and technical buyers looking for a specific connector standard or cable length.

How to choose cables and connectivity hardware

Confirm the connector at both ends

Most cable issues come from a small mismatch between connector type, version or pin layout. Note the exact port on the source device and the target device before ordering, including the USB generation (USB 2.0, USB 3.x, USB-C with or without DisplayPort alt-mode), HDMI version, or DisplayPort version where relevant.

Choose a length that matches the route

A cable that is too short forces equipment placement; one that is too long collects dust and adds signal loss on high-speed standards. Measure the actual route, not the straight-line distance, and add a small margin for routing.

Match the cable rating to the data rate

High-speed standards like Thunderbolt, USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 and HDMI 2.1 only deliver their full performance with cables certified for that rate. A cheaper cable will physically connect but may run at a lower speed or fail intermittently under load.

Plan power separately from data

Power cables and adapters have different ratings (current, voltage, plug type) and should be selected based on the total load they will carry. Mixing data and power into one cable only works for specific standards such as USB-C with Power Delivery.

What to check before buying

  • Which exact connectors and standards are at each end of the cable run?
  • What data rate or power level does the cable need to carry?
  • Is the cable run permanent, or will it be plugged and unplugged frequently?
  • Is shielding or a specific cable jacket needed for the environment (industrial, in-wall, plenum)?
  • Does the setup need a hub, an adapter, or a single direct cable?

Frequently asked questions

What is the difference between USB 3.0, USB 3.1 and USB 3.2?

The USB-IF reorganized its naming, so the same physical port may carry different speeds depending on the host and cable. USB 3.2 Gen 1 (formerly 3.0) runs at 5 Gbps, Gen 2 at 10 Gbps and Gen 2x2 at 20 Gbps over USB-C. The cable, host and device all have to support the higher rate to reach it.

Do I need an active or passive cable?

Passive cables work for most short runs at common data rates. Active cables include a small chip that boosts the signal, allowing longer runs at higher speeds. For Thunderbolt, HDMI 2.1 or DisplayPort over a few meters, an active or fiber-optic cable is usually required.

Will a USB hub slow down my devices?

A hub shares the bandwidth of the upstream port between all connected devices. For keyboards, mice and storage used one at a time, this is rarely noticeable. Running multiple high-speed devices, like external SSDs or capture devices, on a single hub can hit the limit. A powered hub also avoids issues when several devices draw current at once.

What is the right card reader for SD and microSD cards?

For modern SD cards, a UHS-II reader is required to reach the rated speeds; a basic UHS-I reader works but caps the transfer rate. CFexpress and XQD cards need their own specific readers. A multi-slot reader is convenient when several formats are in regular use.

Are all USB-C cables the same?

No. USB-C is a connector, not a single standard. The cable can carry anything from USB 2.0 speed and 3 A power up to Thunderbolt 4 with 100 W Power Delivery and DisplayPort video. Always check the specifications, especially when charging laptops or driving displays.

Can I extend a cable with a coupler?

Couplers work for low-speed cables but introduce a junction that can cause issues at high data rates. For HDMI, DisplayPort and USB above a few meters, an active extender or a single longer cable is more reliable.

components/cables/card-readers.meta_desc_en: Card readers for SD, microSD, CFexpress and CompactFlash. Compare single-slot and multi-slot models with USB-A and USB-C connections.
components/cables/card-readers.seo_desc_en: Standalone card readers for transferring photos, video and other data from memory cards into a PC or notebook. The selection covers single-slot readers for one specific format and multi-slot units that handle SD, microSD, CompactFlash and CFexpress in one device. Choices are sorted by interface so the reader's speed matches the card and the host port.
components/cables/card-readers.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

A card reader connects memory cards to a computer or notebook over USB or another standard interface. For photographers, video producers and IT staff who handle removable media regularly, a dedicated reader is faster and more reliable than the slot built into a notebook chassis.

What this category covers

Single-format readers (SD only, microSD only, CFexpress, CompactFlash), multi-format readers that combine several slots, and small adapter readers used for occasional file transfers.

How to choose a card reader

Match the reader to the fastest card you use

Older USB 2.0 readers cap out around 30 MB/s, well below what modern SD or microSD cards can deliver. For UHS-II SD cards, choose a UHS-II reader with USB 3.x. For CFexpress, only a dedicated CFexpress reader will reach the rated speed.

Choose the host interface

USB-A is universal and works with most PCs and notebooks. USB-C is more common on newer notebooks and runs at higher rates with a Gen 2 host. A reader with both connectors, or a USB-C reader with an A adapter, covers most office setups.

Single-slot or multi-slot

A single-slot reader is compact and rugged for one specific format. A multi-slot reader is convenient when several formats are in use at the same workstation, but the read speed of one slot is sometimes capped when another slot is active.

What to check before buying

  • Which card formats and speed classes will the reader need to handle?
  • Does the host PC have a USB 3.x port to take advantage of the reader's full speed?
  • Is the reader meant for a fixed workstation, a travel kit, or shared use?
  • Does the workflow benefit from a permanent USB cable or a direct-plug stick design?

Frequently asked questions

Why is my card reader slower than the card's rated speed?

The slowest link in the chain sets the rate: card, reader and host. A UHS-II SD card in a UHS-I reader will run at UHS-I speeds. A USB 3.x reader plugged into a USB 2.0 port will run at USB 2.0 speeds. Match all three to reach the card's rated transfer rate.

Do I need a separate reader if my notebook already has an SD slot?

Built-in slots are convenient but often slower and limited to standard SD cards. For UHS-II cards, microSD without an adapter, or CFexpress media, a dedicated external reader is usually faster and avoids wear on the notebook slot.

What is the difference between UHS-I and UHS-II?

UHS-II adds a second row of pins on the SD card and reader, doubling the available bandwidth. A UHS-II card stays compatible with a UHS-I reader, but only at the lower speed.

Are CFexpress and XQD readers interchangeable?

CFexpress Type B and XQD share the same physical connector, and most current CFexpress readers also read XQD cards. CFexpress Type A and Type B are different sizes and need their own readers. Always check the supported standard before ordering.

Can a card reader corrupt data?

A reader does not normally corrupt data on its own. Removing a card while the host is still writing, or losing power mid-transfer, are the common causes of corruption. Always wait for the transfer indicator to clear before pulling the card.

components/cables/data-cables-adapters.meta_desc_en: Data cables and adapters for displays, storage and networking: HDMI, DisplayPort, USB, Thunderbolt and Ethernet for PCs, servers and peripherals.
components/cables/data-cables-adapters.seo_desc_en: Cables and adapters that carry data between PCs, displays, storage devices, peripherals and network equipment. The selection covers HDMI, DisplayPort, USB, Thunderbolt and Ethernet runs, plus the adapters used to bridge mismatched connectors on a single setup. Each option is rated for a specific generation, so the cable matches the speed the host and device negotiate.
components/cables/data-cables-adapters.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Data cables and adapters connect a computer to its displays, peripherals, storage and network. The right cable depends as much on the version of the standard at each end as on the connector itself.

What this category covers

Display cables (HDMI, DisplayPort, DVI), video adapters (HDMI to DisplayPort, USB-C to HDMI), USB cables in all common generations, Thunderbolt cables, and network patch leads.

How to choose a data cable

Identify the standard, not just the connector

Two HDMI cables can look identical but carry very different data rates. HDMI 2.1 is required for 4K at 120 Hz or 8K. DisplayPort 2.1, Thunderbolt 4 and USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 each have their own certified cable specifications. Confirm the version at the source and the display before ordering.

Pick a length matched to the rate

High-speed standards are sensitive to length. A passive HDMI 2.1 cable is reliable for around 3 meters; longer runs need an active or fiber-optic cable. For DisplayPort and USB 3.x, similar limits apply. Plan the route, then pick the shortest cable that does the job.

Use adapters carefully

An adapter can bridge connector mismatches but cannot add features the host does not have. A USB-C to HDMI adapter only works on a USB-C port that supports DisplayPort alt-mode. A passive HDMI to DisplayPort adapter only works in one direction; the other direction needs an active converter.

Key features to compare

Bandwidth and version

Match the version on the cable to the highest version supported on both ends. Buying ahead of the standard rarely pays off because the connector and host need to support it too.

Shielding and build quality

For permanent runs or industrial settings, look for braided shielding, robust strain relief and certified cable assemblies. For desk use, a basic well-rated cable is usually enough.

What to check before buying

  • What exact version of the standard is needed at each end?
  • Will the cable run be permanent, in-wall, or used on a desk?
  • Does the setup actually need an adapter, or would a single direct cable be cleaner?
  • Is signal direction a concern, since some adapters only work one way?

Frequently asked questions

Will any HDMI cable carry 4K video?

Most modern HDMI cables handle 4K at 60 Hz. For 4K at 120 Hz, 8K, or full HDR with high bit depth, the cable needs to be certified for HDMI 2.1 (Ultra High Speed). The TV or monitor and the source device also need to support those modes.

Why does my USB-C cable not charge my notebook?

USB-C cables vary widely in capability. A cable rated only for USB 2.0 data and 3 A power cannot deliver the 60 W or 100 W needed to charge a notebook. Use a cable rated for the wattage of the charger and verify it supports USB Power Delivery.

Is DisplayPort better than HDMI?

For computer monitors, DisplayPort generally supports higher refresh rates at a given bandwidth and is the standard on workstation graphics cards. HDMI is the dominant standard for televisions and consumer audio-video. For most desktop builds, either works; the choice usually depends on the available outputs.

Do I need a Thunderbolt cable, or will USB-C do?

If both ends are Thunderbolt devices and the workload needs the bandwidth (external GPUs, fast external SSDs, high-resolution displays), a Thunderbolt 3 or 4 certified cable is required. For everyday charging and slower data, a regular USB-C cable is enough.

Why does my video adapter only work in one direction?

HDMI and DisplayPort use different signaling. A passive adapter relies on a host that can output the other standard natively. Active converters include a small circuit that translates one signal to the other and work regardless of host capability.

components/cables/power-cables.meta_desc_en: Power cables for PCs, monitors, servers and UPS units. Find IEC C13, C14, C19 leads, country-specific plugs and rated extension leads.
components/cables/power-cables.seo_desc_en: Mains and equipment power cables for desktops, monitors, servers, network gear and Uninterruptible Power Supplies. The selection covers IEC C13 and C19 kettle leads, country-specific wall plugs, jumper cables for rack equipment, and rated extensions. Each cable is rated for a specific current and voltage, so the lead matches the load it will carry.
components/cables/power-cables.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Power cables move mains current from a wall socket or a Power Distribution Unit to the equipment that needs it. Choosing the right cable is mostly about three things: the connector at each end, the country of use, and the current the cable has to carry without overheating.

What this category covers

Computer power cables with C13 connectors, server and high-current cables with C19 connectors, jumper cables for racks (C13 to C14, C19 to C20), country-specific wall plugs and rated extension leads.

How to choose a power cable

Match the connector at the equipment side

Most desktops, monitors and small Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) units use a C13 socket and a C14-equivalent kettle lead. High-current servers, larger UPS units and some Power Distribution Units (PDUs) use C19. The label near the device socket lists the IEC standard.

Choose the right country plug

Power cables ship with regional plugs (Schuko, UK, US, Swiss, Italian, Australian and others). For an international team or shared spare stock, jumper cables that go directly from a PDU to the equipment avoid the country issue altogether.

Check the current rating

A C13 cable is typically rated for 10 A, a C19 cable for 16 A or more. Running a high-load device through an underrated cable is unsafe and can also trip the equipment. The rating is printed on the cable jacket.

Key features to compare

Length and routing

Shorter cables reduce clutter and voltage drop. Longer cables are useful in rack environments where the PDU and the device may sit far apart. Avoid coiling unused length tightly under load.

Shielding and jacket

For office use, a standard PVC jacket is fine. For industrial or high-temperature environments, look for higher-rated jackets, locking connectors or armored cables.

What to check before buying

  • Which exact IEC connector does the equipment use, and how many do you need?
  • What country plug or PDU outlet does the cable have to plug into?
  • What is the maximum current the cable will need to carry continuously?
  • Is a locking connector required to prevent accidental disconnection?
  • Is the route long enough to need a longer cable rather than an extension?

Frequently asked questions

What is a C13 cable?

C13 is the IEC standard for the female connector found on most computer power cables. It plugs into a C14 male inlet on the device. Together this is sometimes called a kettle lead because the same connector was used on early electric kettles.

When do I need a C19 cable instead of C13?

Equipment that draws more than about 10 amps uses the larger C19 inlet, which mates with a C20 plug. This is common on rack servers, larger UPS units and some PDUs. Using C19 cables with the matched outlet gives more headroom and safer operation under continuous load.

Can I use a regular extension cord with a server?

Generic household extension cords are often rated for lower current than rack equipment can draw under load. Use a rated IEC jumper cable, a properly rated PDU, or an extension specifically rated for the equipment's nameplate current.

Why does my power cable feel warm?

A small amount of warmth is normal under heavy load. Hot to the touch suggests the cable is undersized for the current, the contact resistance at the plug or socket is too high, or the cable is coiled tightly under load. Stop the load and check the rating.

Are all C13 cables the same?

Connector-wise yes, but the current rating, conductor cross-section and jacket type vary. A budget 0.75 mm² cable is fine for a monitor; a 1.0 mm² or 1.5 mm² cable is needed for a desktop drawing close to the C13 limit.

components/cables/usb-interface-hubs.meta_desc_en: USB hubs and docking interfaces for desktops and notebooks. Compare USB-A and USB-C hubs, powered models, and small docks for travel.
components/cables/usb-interface-hubs.seo_desc_en: USB hubs and small docking devices that turn a single port on a desktop or notebook into several. The selection covers USB-A and USB-C hubs, bus-powered units for travel, and externally powered hubs for permanent desk use. Listings show the upstream interface, downstream port mix and supported speeds so the hub matches the workload.
components/cables/usb-interface-hubs.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

USB hubs and small interface docks expand a single port on a host machine into several. They are common on desks where a notebook only has one or two USB-C ports, and on workstations where the case ports are not enough for the connected peripherals.

What this category covers

Bus-powered USB-A and USB-C hubs for occasional use, externally powered hubs for permanent desk setups, and small interface hubs that combine USB ports with card readers, Ethernet or display outputs.

How to choose a hub

Decide between bus-powered and externally powered

Bus-powered hubs draw current from the host port and are convenient for travel. They struggle when several devices try to draw current at the same time, especially portable drives. An externally powered hub uses its own adapter and gives each downstream port the full rated current.

Match the upstream version to the workload

A USB 2.0 hub is fine for keyboards, mice and printers. A USB 3.x hub is needed for fast external storage or capture devices. The hub's upstream connection is the shared bandwidth ceiling for everything plugged into it.

Look at the downstream port mix

Some hubs have all USB-A ports; others mix USB-A and USB-C. A few include extras like Gigabit Ethernet, an SD card reader or HDMI output. Pick the mix that fits the day-to-day connections rather than buying the largest port count.

Key features to compare

Power Delivery passthrough

USB-C hubs that support Power Delivery passthrough let the host charge through the hub while it carries data. This is useful for notebooks with only one or two ports.

Per-port switches

Some powered hubs include a switch on each port. This is useful for shared workstations where a device needs to be electrically disconnected without unplugging it.

What to check before buying

  • How many ports do you actually need, and of which types?
  • Will the hub stay on a desk or move between locations?
  • Will any device on the hub draw significant current (external SSD, portable scanner, charging phone)?
  • Does the host machine support Power Delivery, and does the hub need to pass it through?

Frequently asked questions

How many devices can I connect to one USB hub?

The USB specification allows up to 127 devices per host controller, including hubs, but real-world limits are much lower. Bandwidth and power are the practical constraints. Plan for the actual mix of devices, not the theoretical maximum.

Can I daisy-chain hubs?

Yes, hubs can be chained, but every level adds latency and reduces available bandwidth. For more than a handful of devices, an externally powered hub with enough ports is more reliable than chaining smaller hubs.

Will a USB 3.x hub make my USB 2.0 devices faster?

No. The hub does not change the speed of devices plugged into it. A USB 2.0 keyboard or mouse runs at USB 2.0 speeds whether connected to a USB 2.0 or USB 3.x hub. The benefit appears when several USB 3.x devices share the upstream link.

Why does my external drive disconnect on a hub?

Most often this is a power issue: a bus-powered hub cannot supply enough current for the drive. Move the drive to an externally powered hub or a port directly on the host machine. Faulty cables and worn connectors are the next most likely cause.

What is the difference between a hub and a dock?

A hub mainly multiplies USB ports. A dock adds a wider mix of interfaces (display outputs, Ethernet, audio) and is built for permanent desk use, often with its own power supply and a single cable to the notebook. The line between the two has blurred in recent USB-C devices.

components/cases.meta_desc_en: PC cases in ATX, micro-ATX, mini-ITX and rack form factors. Compare drive bays, GPU clearance, airflow and cable management for builds.
components/cases.seo_desc_en: PC and server cases for desktop, workstation and rack-mount builds. The selection covers full tower, mid tower, micro-ATX, mini-ITX and 1U to 4U rack chassis, with attention to drive bay count, graphics card clearance, cooling support and cable routing. Each case is matched to a motherboard form factor and a power supply class so the build fits as planned.
components/cases.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

The case ties the rest of the build together: it sets the form factor, the cooling envelope, and how easy the system is to maintain. A case that fits today's components but leaves no room for cooling or upgrades will limit the build sooner than the parts inside it.

What this category covers

Full-tower, mid-tower, micro-ATX and mini-ITX desktop cases for general builds; small form factor and home theater designs; and 1U through 4U rack chassis for server installations.

How to choose a case

Start with the motherboard form factor

The motherboard sets the minimum case size: ATX, micro-ATX, mini-ITX, E-ATX or a server form factor like SSI EEB. A case that supports a larger board can usually take a smaller one, but not the other way around.

Check graphics card and cooler clearance

Modern graphics cards can be over 350 mm long; modern air coolers can be over 160 mm tall. Check the maximum supported lengths and heights against the parts you intend to install, ideally with a few millimeters of margin for cables.

Plan the storage layout

Count the drive bays you actually need: 2.5-inch SSD trays, 3.5-inch HDD bays, and any front-accessible hot-swap bays for servers. Fewer bays usually means better airflow; more bays add flexibility at the cost of clutter.

Consider airflow and noise

A case with mesh front panels and good fan placement runs cooler and quieter under load than a sealed glass front. For office use, sound-dampened cases are worth specifying. For server racks, airflow direction (front to back) and depth take priority.

Key features to compare

Cable management space

The clearance behind the motherboard tray determines how clean the build looks and how well air moves through the case. A few extra millimeters here makes a real difference during assembly.

Front panel ports

USB-C, USB-A, audio jacks and sometimes a fan controller. For frequently used systems, having modern ports on the front saves time over reaching to the rear.

Tool-less features

Thumb screws, removable drive cages and quick-release side panels speed up service work, especially on systems that get touched often.

What to check before buying

  • Does the motherboard form factor match the case's supported standards?
  • Will the longest graphics card and tallest cooler you plan to install fit?
  • How many drives, and of which form factor, will the case need to hold?
  • What is the airflow plan, and does the case support fans of the size and count you need?
  • For rack use: is the depth correct, and are sliding rails included or sold separately?

Frequently asked questions

How do I know if my graphics card will fit?

Check the maximum graphics card length listed in the case specifications, then compare it to the card length on the GPU spec sheet. Subtract a small margin for power connectors, which often plug in along the long edge. If the case has a front fan in front of the GPU, deduct that fan's depth as well.

Is bigger always better?

Not necessarily. A larger case usually means more airflow and easier cable routing, but also more desk space, weight and cost. Match the size to the parts going inside, with margin for one or two upgrades, rather than buying the largest case you can afford.

What is the difference between mid-tower and full-tower?

Mid-tower cases support standard ATX motherboards and most components. Full-tower cases support extended motherboards (E-ATX, SSI EEB), more drive bays, larger radiators and more demanding cooling setups. Most builds fit comfortably in a mid-tower.

Do server cases really need rails?

Sliding rails are not strictly required, but they make service much easier and reduce wear on the cabling. For any rack server that may be opened more than once after install, rails are worth the small extra cost.

Does a quiet case really make a difference?

Yes, for office and home environments. Sound-dampened panels, foam-lined sides and well-routed fan paths can reduce noise by several decibels under normal load. The remaining noise floor is set by the components themselves, especially the GPU and CPU coolers.

components/fans-cooling-systems.meta_desc_en: Case fans, CPU coolers, AIO and custom liquid cooling for desktops, workstations and servers. Pick the right airflow, noise level and mount size.
components/fans-cooling-systems.seo_desc_en: Cooling parts for desktop, workstation and server builds: case fans, CPU air coolers, all-in-one liquid coolers, custom loop components, and replacement fans for rack chassis. Listings show fan size, mounting type, airflow and static pressure, noise level, and the socket support of each cooler so the choice fits both the case and the processor.
components/fans-cooling-systems.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Cooling decides how much real performance the rest of the build can deliver. A fast CPU or GPU that runs into thermal limits will throttle, regardless of its specifications. The right cooling combination keeps the system at a safe temperature under load and at a reasonable noise level the rest of the time.

What this category covers

Case fans in 80, 92, 120, 140 and 200 mm sizes; CPU air coolers from low-profile to dual-tower designs; closed-loop all-in-one (AIO) liquid coolers; custom loop components such as pumps, blocks and radiators; and high static-pressure server-grade fans for rack equipment.

How to choose cooling

Size the cooler to the actual power draw

Match the cooler's rated capacity to the CPU's real-world power draw under sustained load, not just to the marketed Thermal Design Power (TDP). A cooler rated at 150 W will struggle on a chip that pulls 200 W under all-core work.

Choose air or liquid based on the case and the workload

Air coolers are simple, reliable and quiet at moderate loads. AIO liquid coolers move heat away from the CPU socket and can handle higher sustained power, at the cost of more components that can fail and slightly higher noise from the pump. For most office and workstation builds, a quality air cooler is enough.

Distinguish airflow fans from static-pressure fans

Airflow fans move large volumes of air through open spaces and suit case intakes and exhausts. Static-pressure fans push air through restrictive surfaces (radiators, fine dust filters, dense heatsinks) and suit AIO and custom loop radiators. Using the wrong type makes the system louder for the same temperature.

Key features to compare

Fan size and mounting

Larger fans move more air at lower speeds and so run more quietly than smaller fans for the same airflow. Server fans tend to be smaller (40 to 80 mm) and faster, which makes them louder by design.

Bearing type

Sleeve bearings are cheap and quiet but wear faster, especially when mounted horizontally. Fluid dynamic and magnetic levitation bearings last longer and stay quiet over time.

PWM versus voltage control

Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) fans use a four-pin connector and can be ramped smoothly by the motherboard. Three-pin fans run at a speed set by voltage. PWM is the more flexible option for modern boards.

What to check before buying

  • Does the CPU cooler support the motherboard's CPU socket?
  • Will the cooler fit in the case (height for air, radiator size for AIO)?
  • Is the fan size matched to the case mounts (the most common standard is 120 mm)?
  • Does the use case prioritize low noise, high airflow under load, or both?
  • For replacements: does the new fan match the existing connector type (3-pin or 4-pin PWM)?

Frequently asked questions

Air cooling or liquid cooling for a workstation?

For most workstations, a high-quality dual-tower air cooler matches the cooling performance of a 240 mm AIO with fewer points of failure. Liquid cooling makes more sense when the CPU power draw is very high, when the case has limited clearance for a tall air cooler, or when the build aesthetic calls for it.

How many case fans do I need?

A balanced setup usually has at least one intake at the front and one exhaust at the rear. Adding a top exhaust helps when the system runs warm or includes a top-mounted radiator. More fans only help if the case has good filtering and the fan curve is tuned; otherwise the noise rises faster than the temperature drops.

Will an AIO cooler last as long as an air cooler?

A well-built AIO has a typical lifespan of five to seven years, limited by the pump and the fluid. A quality air cooler can run for ten years or more, with the only worn part being the fans. For long-life office systems, air cooling is generally lower-risk.

Why are my server fans so loud?

Server chassis use small, high-static-pressure fans that spin at high speed by design. The rate ramps with internal temperature. If the noise is excessive, check the inlet temperature, clear any dust filters, and verify the fan curve in the BMC is not set to a more aggressive profile than the workload requires.

Can I mix different fan brands and models?

Yes, mechanically and electrically the connectors are standard. The downside is that each model has its own noise profile and ramp behaviour, so a mixed set rarely sounds as clean as a matched one. For balanced setups, use the same model for fans on the same surface.

components/keyboards-mice.meta_desc_en: Keyboards, mice, mousepads and stylus pens for office, productivity and creative workstations. Compare wired, wireless and ergonomic options.
components/keyboards-mice.seo_desc_en: Input devices for desktop and notebook setups: keyboards, mice, mousepads, wrist rests, digitizers and stylus pens. The group covers everyday office hardware and the more specialized devices used by designers, illustrators and engineering teams. Subcategories separate each device type so the right combination can be put together for a specific desk or workflow.
components/keyboards-mice.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Input devices are the parts of the build that get touched all day. The right keyboard and mouse, plus a stable surface and an appropriate pen if needed, have a bigger effect on daily comfort and productivity than the headline specifications suggest.

What this category covers

Office, productivity and gaming keyboards; standard, ergonomic and presenter mice; mousepads, desk pads and wrist rests; and digitizers and stylus pens used with pen displays and graphics tablets.

Who this category is for

IT teams equipping a fleet of office workstations, system builders finishing a new build, and individual buyers replacing worn input devices or upgrading to ergonomic models.

How to choose input devices

Match the device to the actual work

Heavy text input benefits from a full-size keyboard with a comfortable key feel. Spreadsheet and finance work justifies a separate numeric keypad. Creative work calls for a precise mouse and often a pen tablet. Gaming and competitive work has its own set of priorities around switch type, polling rate and weight.

Decide between wired and wireless

Wired devices remove battery management and pairing issues; wireless devices clean up the desk and travel better. Modern wireless mice and keyboards have low latency that suits most non-competitive use, especially with a dedicated USB receiver rather than Bluetooth alone.

Consider ergonomics from the start

Split keyboards, vertical mice and adjustable wrist rests reduce strain on long days at a desk. They take a few days to adapt to but pay back in comfort and reduced repetitive strain.

What to check before buying

  • How many hours per day will the device be used, and for what kind of work?
  • Is wired, wireless with receiver, or Bluetooth the right connection for the desk and host?
  • Does the user already have ergonomic preferences, or is this a chance to switch?
  • Will the device be shared between hosts (KVM, dock) and need multi-host pairing?

Frequently asked questions

Wired or wireless for office use?

Both work well. Wired removes batteries and pairing entirely, which suits shared desks. Wireless clears the desk and is easier to move with a notebook. Pick the connection that fits the desk environment more than the device specifications.

Should everyone in the office have the same keyboard and mouse?

Standardizing reduces support load and makes spares interchangeable, which is a real benefit for IT. Allowing individual choices for power users (developers, designers, finance) tends to pay back in productivity and comfort. A middle path, with a default kit and an approved upgrade list, works in most teams.

Do mechanical keyboards last longer than membrane ones?

Mechanical keyboards typically last tens of millions of keypresses per switch, well beyond a typical office membrane keyboard. The trade-off is higher cost and, depending on the switch, more noise. For heavy typists, the longer life and key feel often justify the price.

Why do my wireless devices have lag sometimes?

Two common causes: a busy 2.4 GHz environment with many wireless devices, or a low battery. Move the receiver to a closer USB port (a short extension helps), avoid USB 3.x ports next to the receiver if possible, and replace or recharge the battery when the indicator drops.

What is a digitizer used for?

Digitizers and stylus pens are used with pen displays, graphics tablets and pen-enabled notebooks for drawing, annotation, digital signatures and precise selection. They give pressure sensitivity and finer control than a mouse for any kind of freehand work.

components/keyboards-mice/digitizers-sty.meta_desc_en: Digitizer pens and replacement styli for graphics tablets, pen displays and pen-enabled notebooks. Compare protocols, pressure levels and tip wear.
components/keyboards-mice/digitizers-sty.seo_desc_en: Digitizer pens, replacement nibs and accessories for graphics tablets, pen displays and pen-enabled notebooks. The selection covers active pens with pressure sensitivity for drawing and design, simple capacitive styli for navigation and signature capture, and replacement tips that wear out with daily use. Each pen is paired with a specific digitizer protocol, so the listings group items by host compatibility rather than by appearance.
components/keyboards-mice/digitizers-sty.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

A digitizer pen is paired with a specific protocol and host device, not just a screen. Choosing the wrong pen for a tablet or display means it either does not register at all or loses pressure sensitivity. The category lists pens by compatible protocol so the match is straightforward.

What this category covers

Active pens for major pen-display and tablet protocols, replacement nibs in different hardness ratings, capacitive styli for general touchscreens, and small accessories such as pen holders and grip rings.

How to choose a digitizer or stylus

Match the pen to the host protocol

Pen displays and graphics tablets use specific digitizer protocols (for example Wacom EMR, AES, MPP). The pen has to match the protocol of the host display or tablet. A pen meant for one protocol will not work on another, regardless of how similar the tip looks.

Check pressure levels and tilt support

Lower-cost pens may report 1024 or 2048 pressure levels; modern pens for design work usually report 4096 or 8192. Tilt support adds a second axis that some painting and sketching software uses for natural strokes. For signing and basic note-taking, fewer pressure levels are fine.

Consider how often nibs wear

Daily use wears the nib down over weeks to months, depending on the screen surface. Buying replacement nibs in the same order as the pen avoids downtime when the first set is worn.

What to check before buying

  • What is the exact protocol of the host tablet, pen display or notebook?
  • Will the pen be used for drawing (where pressure and tilt matter) or for navigation and signing (where they do not)?
  • Does the pen need a battery, or is it powered through the screen?
  • Are spare nibs included, and is a replacement set available separately?

Frequently asked questions

Will any stylus work on my pen display?

No. Pen displays use a specific digitizer protocol, and the pen must match. A capacitive stylus made for general touchscreens will not give pressure sensitivity on a pen display, and an active pen for one protocol will not work on a tablet that uses a different one.

How long does a pen nib last?

It depends on grip pressure, screen texture and hours of use. A typical nib lasts from a few weeks of heavy daily drawing to several months of lighter use. Replacement nibs usually come in packs of 5 to 10.

Do I need a pen with a battery?

Most modern pen-display protocols power the pen wirelessly through the screen, so no battery is needed. Some pens use a small replaceable battery or an internal rechargeable cell to add features like programmable buttons or higher report rates.

What are pressure levels in practice?

The pressure level count is the resolution of the pressure axis the pen reports. The visible difference between 4096 and 8192 levels is small for most users; the larger gain is between an unpressured stylus and any active pen. For professional design work, more levels and tilt support add nuance.

Can a stylus damage my screen?

A pen with the correct nib for the screen will not damage it. Damage usually comes from a worn-down nib pressing the metal core onto the screen, or from grit caught between nib and screen. Replace nibs when they show clear wear and clean the screen regularly.

components/keyboards-mice/keyboards.meta_desc_en: Compare office, mechanical and wireless keyboards. Pick the right layout, switch type, connectivity and ergonomic features for daily use.
components/keyboards-mice/keyboards.seo_desc_en: Keyboards for office work, productivity, gaming and shared workstations. The selection covers full-size, tenkeyless, compact and ergonomic split layouts; membrane, scissor-switch and mechanical keyboards; and wired, wireless and Bluetooth multi-host options. Listings show layout, switch type, connection and any backlight features so the choice fits the desk and the user.
components/keyboards-mice/keyboards.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

A keyboard is the device most office users touch the longest each day. Layout, switch feel and connectivity matter more than headline features. The category lists models by layout and switch type so the comparison stays focused on what affects daily use.

What this category covers

Full-size keyboards (104 to 108 keys with a numeric pad), tenkeyless and 75 percent compact designs that save desk space, ergonomic split keyboards, and dedicated number pads sold separately.

How to choose a keyboard

Choose the layout first

Full-size keeps the numeric pad for finance, accounting and data work. Tenkeyless removes the pad, brings the mouse closer to the body and reduces shoulder strain. Compact 75 percent and 65 percent designs save more space and are popular with developers and travelers.

Match the switch to typing style

Membrane and scissor-switch keyboards are quiet, low-profile and inexpensive, suited to shared offices. Mechanical switches give clearer feedback, last longer, and come in linear, tactile and clicky variants. Try a switch tester or a colleague's keyboard before committing for a long-term setup.

Pick the connection

Wired USB removes battery and pairing concerns and gives the lowest latency. Wireless with a 2.4 GHz dongle is reliable for most desks. Bluetooth keyboards with multi-host pairing are useful when one keyboard needs to switch between a desktop, a notebook and a tablet.

Key features to compare

Backlight

Single-color or RGB backlight helps in low-light environments. For office buyers, the value is mostly visibility on a dim desk, not the color customization.

Programmable keys and macros

Macro keys and software-defined layers add value for power users who repeat the same actions. Most office users will use the defaults, so this is a per-user preference rather than a fleet requirement.

Build quality and keycap material

An aluminium top plate and PBT keycaps last longer and resist shine from finger oils. ABS keycaps are more common at lower prices and become smooth with heavy use over time.

What to check before buying

  • What language layout is needed (US ANSI, UK ISO, Latvian, Russian, others)?
  • Is a numeric pad needed for the daily workload?
  • Is shared office noise a constraint that rules out clicky switches?
  • Does the keyboard need to switch between several hosts?
  • Is a wrist rest included or sold separately?

Frequently asked questions

Are mechanical keyboards better for typing?

For users who type heavily all day, mechanical switches give clearer feedback, last longer and reduce typing fatigue once they are matched to typing style. The difference is real but personal; some users prefer the quieter, lower-profile feel of scissor-switch keyboards.

What does tenkeyless mean?

Tenkeyless (TKL) keyboards remove the numeric pad on the right side, leaving around 87 keys instead of 104. The space saved lets the mouse sit closer to the body, which is more comfortable on long days.

How loud is a clicky mechanical switch in an open office?

Clicky switches (such as Cherry MX Blue and similar) are noticeably louder than membrane keyboards and can be disruptive in shared offices. Linear and tactile switches are much quieter. For shared spaces, choose linear switches or scissor-switch keyboards.

Why does my keyboard miss keypresses?

Causes include a worn switch, debris under the keycap, an unreliable wireless connection, or a USB hub that drops short signals. Try the keyboard on a different host or port, clean under the affected key, and replace batteries if it is wireless.

Are gaming keyboards good for office use?

Many gaming keyboards are well-built mechanical models that work fine for office use. The downsides are usually appearance (heavy RGB lighting, gamer styling) and noise, depending on the switch. A gaming keyboard with linear switches and a calm finish is often a strong office keyboard.

components/keyboards-mice/mouse.meta_desc_en: Compare office, ergonomic and wireless mice. Find the right shape, sensor, button count and connection for daily desk use or travel.
components/keyboards-mice/mouse.seo_desc_en: Computer mice for office, productivity, design and travel use. The category covers standard symmetrical mice, ergonomic vertical and shaped designs, compact mobile mice for notebooks, and trackballs for users who prefer not to move the wrist. Listings show shape, sensor accuracy, button count and connection so the right model is easy to select.
components/keyboards-mice/mouse.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

A mouse is one of the cheapest changes that can make daily computer use more comfortable. The right shape, weight and connection often matter more than headline sensor specifications.

What this category covers

Standard right-handed and ambidextrous office mice; vertical and other ergonomic shapes designed to reduce wrist strain; compact travel mice; trackballs; and presenter mice with built-in laser pointers.

How to choose a mouse

Start with the hand and the grip

Mice come in different sizes and shapes for palm, claw and fingertip grips. The most common reason for discomfort is a mouse that is too small for the hand. Check the listed dimensions against the user's hand size where possible.

Decide between standard and ergonomic

Vertical and shaped ergonomic mice put the wrist in a more neutral position, which helps users who feel strain after long sessions. The trade-off is a short adaptation period. For users without symptoms, a well-fitted standard mouse is fine.

Pick the connection

Wired mice are reliable and need no batteries. 2.4 GHz wireless with a USB receiver is the most common modern option. Bluetooth mice are useful for travel and for switching between hosts, with slightly higher latency that is rarely noticeable in office use.

Key features to compare

Sensor and DPI range

Office work runs comfortably in the 800 to 1600 DPI range. Higher DPI is useful on high-resolution displays and for fine work in design software. Marketing numbers above 8000 DPI rarely change daily use.

Button count and side buttons

Side buttons mapped to back, forward and copy or paste shortcuts speed up daily work. For specialized workflows, mice with more programmable buttons add real value once the user invests time in mapping them.

Battery life and charging

Wireless mice with replaceable AA batteries last months. Rechargeable mice with USB-C charging avoid disposable batteries and integrate with a USB-C desk setup. Both are reasonable; the choice is mostly about personal preference and office disposal policy.

What to check before buying

  • What is the user's grip style and hand size?
  • Is wrist strain a current issue, or is this a routine replacement?
  • Will the mouse travel with a notebook or stay on one desk?
  • Does the user need multi-host switching across desktop and notebook?
  • Is silent clicking required for shared spaces?

Frequently asked questions

Are vertical mice worth it?

For users who feel wrist strain, yes. The vertical position is more neutral and reduces forearm rotation. New users need a few days to adapt; after that, most do not switch back. For users without strain, a standard mouse fits more naturally.

How high a DPI do I really need?

Office work is comfortable between 800 and 1600 DPI. Designers and users on 4K displays may prefer 2400 to 3200 DPI. Above that, the gain is mostly marketing for non-gaming use.

Wired or wireless for office work?

Both work well. Wireless with a 2.4 GHz dongle has the cleanest desk and very low latency. Wired removes the battery question entirely. Pick whichever fits the desk and host setup.

What is the difference between a mouse and a trackball?

A trackball stays in one place on the desk and is moved with the thumb or fingertips on a ball. It removes the need to swing the wrist and is useful in tight desks, on uneven surfaces, or for users with shoulder or wrist problems. The learning curve is real but short.

Why does my wireless mouse stutter?

Common causes include low battery, a busy 2.4 GHz environment, USB 3.x interference near the receiver, or a worn sensor on a reflective surface. Replace the battery, move the receiver to a different USB port (a short extension often helps), and use a mousepad on shiny desks.

components/keyboards-mice/mousepads-desk.meta_desc_en: Mousepads, full-size desk pads and wrist rests for keyboards and mice. Compare materials, sizes and surface types for office and creative use.
components/keyboards-mice/mousepads-desk.seo_desc_en: Surface and support accessories for the desk: mousepads, large desk pads that cover keyboard and mouse together, and wrist rests for both keyboards and mice. The selection covers cloth, hard, hybrid and leather-effect surfaces in standard and oversized sizes, plus gel and memory-foam wrist rests in different lengths to match the height of common keyboards.
components/keyboards-mice/mousepads-desk.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

A good mouse surface and a stable wrist support change how a desk feels day to day. They protect the desk surface, keep the mouse tracking consistently, and reduce the small repetitive strain that builds up over a working week.

What this category covers

Standard mousepads in fabric and hard surfaces, oversized desk pads that sit under both the keyboard and mouse, and wrist rests in different materials and sizes for keyboards and mice.

How to choose a desk surface or wrist rest

Decide between mousepad and desk pad

A standard mousepad sits under the mouse only and is easy to swap or move. A desk pad covers most of the working area, protects the desk underneath, and keeps the keyboard and mouse on the same surface, which is helpful on glass or high-gloss desks where a mouse sensor can struggle.

Pick a surface that suits the mouse

Cloth surfaces are forgiving and work with most modern optical sensors. Hard plastic and aluminium surfaces give a faster glide and last longer but are less comfortable to rest the wrist on. Hybrid surfaces try to combine both. For the desk, glass and high-gloss laminates often need a pad for reliable sensor tracking.

Match wrist rests to the keyboard and mouse

Wrist rests should sit at the same height as the front edge of the keyboard or mouse so the wrist stays neutral. Gel rests are softer and good for long sessions; memory foam keeps its shape over time; firm leather-effect rests are easy to clean in shared offices.

What to check before buying

  • How large is the working area, and will the pad fit alongside other items?
  • Does the desk surface cause sensor problems that a pad would fix?
  • Is wrist support already provided by the keyboard or mouse design?
  • Is the pad meant for a single user or a shared desk that needs cleaning?

Frequently asked questions

Do I really need a mousepad with a modern optical mouse?

On most matte desk surfaces, no. Glass, gloss laminate and patterned wood can confuse optical sensors and cause skipping. A pad also gives a consistent surface and protects the desk from wear. For mixed environments, a basic pad is a small investment that removes a class of problems.

Cloth or hard mousepad?

Cloth is comfortable, quiet, and easier to wash. Hard pads give a faster, more consistent glide and last longer but feel cooler under the wrist and can be louder. Most office users prefer cloth; users who do precise design work sometimes prefer the consistency of hard surfaces.

How big should a desk pad be?

A common size is around 800 by 300 mm, which fits a standard keyboard and a comfortable mouse range. Larger pads (900 to 1200 mm) suit wide ultrawide-monitor setups or users who like to keep notepads and a mouse on the pad together.

Are wrist rests good for everyone?

They help users who rest the wrist heavily on the desk while typing or mousing. For users who keep the wrist floating above the desk, a wrist rest is mostly cosmetic. The most important factor is keeping the wrist in a neutral position, which the right desk and chair height also affect.

How do I clean a fabric mousepad?

Most fabric pads can be hand-washed in lukewarm water with mild soap, rinsed and air-dried flat. Avoid the washing machine and tumble dryer, which can damage the rubber backing. Replace the pad once the surface becomes shiny and the sensor tracking gets unreliable.

components/kvm.meta_desc_en: KVM switches for sharing keyboard, video and mouse between PCs and servers. Compare port count, video resolution and remote access options.
components/kvm.seo_desc_en: Keyboard, Video and Mouse (KVM) switches that let one set of peripherals control several computers or servers. The selection covers desktop KVMs for two to four hosts, rack KVMs for server rooms, and IP-based units for remote access. Listings show port count, supported video resolution, USB peripheral support and any audio passthrough.
components/kvm.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

A KVM switch consolidates a keyboard, mouse and one or more displays across several computers, so a user can move between hosts without changing cables. KVMs save desk space, simplify cabling, and in server-room contexts give one console for an entire rack.

What this category covers

Desktop KVMs that switch between two to four PCs over USB and HDMI or DisplayPort, multi-monitor KVMs that switch two or three displays at once, rack-mounted KVMs with built-in or external consoles, and IP-based KVMs that allow remote access to a server's console over the network.

How to choose a KVM switch

Count hosts and displays

The basic spec is the number of hosts (2, 4, 8, 16) and the number of displays per host (single, dual, triple). Dual-monitor KVMs are common for office workstations; quad-display models exist but raise the price quickly.

Match the video standard and resolution

Pick a KVM rated for the actual resolution and refresh rate of the displays. A KVM that only supports 4K at 30 Hz will drop frames on a 4K 60 Hz panel. For modern displays at 4K 120 Hz or 8K, the cost rises and the cable specifications get strict.

Consider USB peripheral support

Basic KVMs pass through the keyboard and mouse only. Better units include extra USB ports for printers, webcams or storage, sometimes with USB 3.x speeds. For desks that share peripherals heavily, this is the feature that decides daily comfort.

Decide if remote access matters

IP-based KVMs let an administrator control a server's console from anywhere on the network, which is essential for headless or remote sites. Local KVMs cost less and are simpler to set up but require physical access.

Key features to compare

Switching method

Front-panel buttons, keyboard hotkeys and mouse-edge switching all coexist on different models. Hotkeys are usually fastest for daily use; physical buttons are most reliable for shared desks.

EDID handling

A good KVM holds the display's EDID information for each host so the operating system does not rearrange windows when the user switches away and back. Cheap KVMs sometimes skip this and cause window-shuffling issues.

What to check before buying

  • How many hosts and how many displays per host need to be switched?
  • What is the highest video resolution and refresh rate that needs to pass through?
  • Are USB peripherals more important than video, or vice versa?
  • Is remote access over the network a current or future requirement?
  • Will the KVM sit on a desk, in a rack, or both?

Frequently asked questions

Will a KVM switch slow down my devices?

A well-built KVM is transparent for keyboard and mouse use, with latency lower than human perception. Video pass-through is also transparent up to the rated resolution and refresh rate. Cheaper units may add small delays in USB peripheral switching after a host change.

Why does my window layout shuffle when I switch hosts?

This usually happens when the KVM does not present the same EDID to every host. The operating system thinks the display is gone and rearranges windows. KVMs with EDID emulation hold the EDID per host and avoid this behaviour.

Can I use one KVM for both Windows and macOS or Linux?

Yes, KVMs are operating-system agnostic at the hardware level. Some advanced features like specific hotkey behaviour or audio passthrough work better with the manufacturer's drivers, which may only ship for Windows. Verify the feature list against each operating system in use.

Do I need a separate KVM cable, or can I use my existing ones?

Most modern KVMs use standard HDMI, DisplayPort and USB cables, which can be replaced with quality off-the-shelf cables. Older or specialty KVMs sometimes use a combined connector that bundles video, USB and audio, in which case the manufacturer's cables are required.

What is an IP KVM used for?

IP KVMs let an administrator log into a server's keyboard, video and mouse from anywhere on the network, even before the operating system has booted. They are essential for remote servers and out-of-hours support, and are often paired with a serial console for full out-of-band access.

components/memory-ram.meta_desc_en: Memory modules for desktops, notebooks, workstations and servers. Compare DDR4 and DDR5, capacity, speed, latency and ECC support.
components/memory-ram.seo_desc_en: Random Access Memory (RAM) modules for desktop, notebook, workstation and server platforms. The category covers DDR4 and DDR5 memory in common capacities and speeds, with consumer, workstation and server-class kits. Subcategories separate desktop DIMMs from notebook SODIMMs so the form factor matches the host before specifications such as speed, latency and ECC support are compared.
components/memory-ram.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Memory affects how much work a system can hold in flight: open applications, browser tabs, virtual machines, large datasets. The right capacity and speed is more important than the brand on the heatspreader, and compatibility depends on the platform.

What this category covers

Desktop and workstation DIMMs in DDR4 and DDR5, notebook SODIMMs for the same generations, and server kits with Error-Correcting Code (ECC) and registered or load-reduced configurations. Subcategories sort by form factor.

How to choose memory

Confirm the platform's memory generation

DDR4 and DDR5 are not interchangeable, and the slots are keyed differently to prevent installing the wrong type. Check the motherboard manual or the existing module's label before ordering replacements or an upgrade.

Pick capacity for the workload

16 GB suits everyday office work and light development. 32 GB covers heavier multitasking, virtual machines, and most creative software. 64 GB and above are common for engineering, simulation, large datasets and databases.

Match speed to what the platform supports

The motherboard, CPU and module all need to support a given speed, or the system runs at the lowest common rate. The motherboard's QVL lists tested kits at their advertised speeds. Faster than supported usually drops back to a slower JEDEC profile.

Key features to compare

Capacity and module count

Two matched modules in dual channel are faster than a single larger module on most platforms. Some platforms support quad channel with four matched modules. Mixed kits often work but may run at lower speeds.

ECC and reliability

ECC memory corrects single-bit errors silently and is the right choice for servers, workstations running long simulations and any system handling critical data. ECC support requires both the CPU and the motherboard to enable it.

What to check before buying

  • Is the platform DDR4 or DDR5, and is the form factor DIMM (desktop) or SODIMM (notebook)?
  • What is the maximum module size the motherboard supports per slot?
  • Does the workload benefit from larger capacity or higher speed?
  • Is ECC required, and does the platform support it?

Frequently asked questions

Can I mix different RAM kits?

Mechanically yes, but the system runs all modules at the lowest common speed and timings, and stability is not guaranteed. For best results, buy a matched kit sized for the eventual capacity, or replace all modules together.

How much RAM is enough?

16 GB is a comfortable minimum for current office work, 32 GB suits heavier multitasking and creative software, and 64 GB or more is standard for engineering, simulation and virtualization workloads. Servers and database hosts often start at 128 GB or higher.

Does faster RAM make a noticeable difference?

For most office work, the difference between baseline and high-speed memory is small. Workloads that depend on memory bandwidth (some games, simulation, content creation) see clearer gains. The main rule is to use a speed the platform actually supports.

What is the difference between ECC and non-ECC memory?

ECC adds an extra chip per module that corrects single-bit errors transparently. It is required for systems that must run for long periods without silent data corruption, such as servers and engineering workstations. Most desktop platforms do not support ECC.

Why does my new RAM run slower than advertised?

Memory often boots at a JEDEC default speed and only reaches the rated speed once an XMP or EXPO profile is enabled in the BIOS. Some modules also fall back to a lower speed if the motherboard or CPU does not support the advertised rate.

components/memory-ram/desktop-dimm.meta_desc_en: Desktop DIMM memory in DDR4 and DDR5. Compare capacity, speed, latency and dual-channel kits for PC and workstation builds.
components/memory-ram/desktop-dimm.seo_desc_en: Full-size DIMM memory modules for desktop and workstation motherboards. The selection covers DDR4 kits for older platforms and DDR5 kits for current ones, in single modules and matched dual-channel pairs. Listings show capacity per module, total kit size, JEDEC and XMP/EXPO speeds, and CAS latency so the comparison stays accurate.
components/memory-ram/desktop-dimm.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Desktop DIMMs are the full-size memory modules used in tower PCs and workstation motherboards. They are physically larger than notebook SODIMMs and use a different connector, so the two are not interchangeable.

What this category covers

DDR4 modules at common speeds for older desktop platforms and DDR5 modules for current ones, including non-ECC kits for consumer use and unbuffered ECC kits for entry-level workstations.

How to choose desktop memory

Confirm DDR4 or DDR5 from the motherboard

The slot type, chipset and CPU all decide whether the board takes DDR4 or DDR5. The DIMM slots are keyed differently for each generation, so a DDR5 module physically will not fit a DDR4 slot. Check the motherboard manual or the printed label on an existing module if uncertain.

Buy as a matched kit when possible

Memory works best in dual-channel mode with two identical modules. Single-module kits work but lose memory bandwidth. Adding a third module of a different brand or speed often forces the system to a slower common speed.

Use the motherboard's QVL for high-speed kits

Higher-speed DDR5 (above 6000 MT/s) and tight-timing DDR4 only run at advertised speed on motherboards that have validated the kit. The motherboard's QVL is the authoritative reference. Kits not on the list often boot at a lower speed.

Key features to compare

Capacity per module and total kit size

Modules come in 8, 16, 32 and 48 GB sizes for DDR5, and similar steps for DDR4. The motherboard manual lists the maximum capacity per slot. Filling all slots with smaller modules usually runs at lower speeds than two larger modules.

Speed and latency

Speed is in MT/s (megatransfers per second). CAS latency (CL) is the response time in clock cycles. A higher speed with slightly higher CL often performs as well as a lower speed with tight CL. The platform's supported speed is the first constraint.

Heat spreaders and clearance

Tall heat spreaders can interfere with large CPU coolers. For air coolers with a wide footprint, low-profile DIMMs are a safer choice; check the cooler manufacturer's clearance figures.

What to check before buying

  • Does the motherboard take DDR4 or DDR5?
  • What is the maximum capacity per slot, and the maximum speed officially supported?
  • Is the chosen kit on the motherboard's QVL?
  • Will the heat spreader fit under the planned CPU cooler?

Frequently asked questions

Can I install DDR5 in a DDR4 motherboard?

No. The slots are keyed differently and the modules will not seat in the wrong slot. Confirm the generation from the motherboard manual before ordering.

Should I buy two 16 GB or one 32 GB module?

Two 16 GB modules in dual channel are usually faster than a single 32 GB module of the same speed, because the memory controller can read and write both channels in parallel. The single-module option only makes sense as a stepping stone to a future kit.

How do I enable the rated speed?

Most kits boot at a slower JEDEC default speed. Enable the XMP profile (Intel) or EXPO profile (AMD) in the BIOS to run at the rated speed. If the system becomes unstable, drop one step in speed or load the QVL-recommended profile.

Does memory speed matter for office work?

Not much. The difference between baseline DDR5 5600 MT/s and high-end 7200 MT/s is small for office software. Capacity is the more important factor for everyday use; speed matters more in games, simulation and content creation.

What does CAS latency mean in practice?

CAS latency is the number of clock cycles between a request and the first piece of data. Combined with the speed, it determines actual response time in nanoseconds. Comparing speed alone or CAS alone can mislead; the combination is what matters.

components/memory-ram/notebook-sodim.meta_desc_en: Notebook SODIMM memory upgrades in DDR4 and DDR5. Compare capacity, speed and dual-channel kits for laptops and small form factor systems.
components/memory-ram/notebook-sodim.seo_desc_en: Small Outline DIMM (SODIMM) memory modules for notebooks, mini PCs and small form factor systems. The selection covers DDR4 and DDR5 SODIMMs in common capacities, both as single replacement modules and as matched dual-channel kits where the host has two slots. Listings show capacity, speed and CAS latency for direct comparison.
components/memory-ram/notebook-sodim.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Notebook memory uses the smaller SODIMM form factor and is not interchangeable with desktop DIMMs. Many notebooks have one or two slots, sometimes with one slot occupied and one free, which makes upgrading to dual channel one of the cheapest performance gains available.

What this category covers

DDR4 SODIMMs for notebooks released up to and including current platforms that retained DDR4, and DDR5 SODIMMs for newer notebooks and small form factor systems. Modules are organized by capacity and speed.

How to choose notebook memory

Confirm the notebook is upgradable

Some thin notebooks, particularly Ultrabooks and many Apple-branded systems, have memory soldered to the mainboard and cannot be upgraded. Check the manufacturer's specification page or service manual for the exact model before ordering.

Match the generation and speed

The notebook's existing module label or the manufacturer's specifications list the supported generation (DDR4 or DDR5) and the maximum speed. A faster module will usually run at the slower supported speed; a slower module will hold the system back.

Use both slots in pairs

If the notebook has two slots and one is empty, adding an identical second module enables dual channel and gives a noticeable bandwidth gain. If both slots are occupied with smaller modules, replace both with a matched kit at the desired total capacity.

Key features to compare

Capacity per module

Common notebook capacities are 8, 16 and 32 GB per SODIMM. The motherboard or notebook manufacturer lists the maximum supported per slot.

Speed and JEDEC profiles

SODIMMs typically run at JEDEC profiles without XMP or EXPO. The motherboard auto-detects the speed and applies the timings.

Voltage

Most current SODIMMs run at 1.2 V (DDR4) or 1.1 V (DDR5). Older 1.35 V modules exist for some platforms and are not interchangeable in newer notebooks.

What to check before buying

  • Does the notebook actually have user-accessible memory slots?
  • How many slots are present, and how many are already occupied?
  • What is the maximum capacity and speed the model supports?
  • Is the existing module DDR4 or DDR5, and at what voltage?

Frequently asked questions

Can I upgrade memory in any notebook?

No. Many thin notebooks have memory soldered to the mainboard and cannot be upgraded. Check the manufacturer's service manual or specifications page for the exact model. If memory is upgradable, it will be listed as a SODIMM slot count, sometimes with maximum capacity per slot.

How do I know what speed my notebook supports?

The notebook's specification sheet lists the supported memory generation and the maximum speed. The label on an existing module also shows its speed. The system will run new memory at the lower of the two: the module's rated speed or the platform's maximum.

Can I mix two different SODIMMs in one notebook?

Mechanically yes if both are the same generation. The system will run at the slower module's speed and timings, and may not enable dual channel if the capacities differ significantly. For best results, use a matched pair.

Will more RAM make my notebook faster?

If the existing memory is a bottleneck (frequent disk swapping, browser tabs reloading, slow virtual machines), more memory makes a noticeable difference. If memory use stays well below the installed amount, more RAM does not improve speed.

Does notebook memory wear out?

Memory chips themselves rarely wear out under normal use. Failures are more often caused by heat, voltage issues or mechanical damage from poor reseating. A notebook that becomes unstable under load is more likely to have a thermal or power problem than a memory wear issue.

components/motherboards.meta_desc_en: Motherboards for AMD and Intel platforms in ATX, micro-ATX and mini-ITX. Compare chipsets, sockets, PCIe layout, M.2 slots and connectivity.
components/motherboards.seo_desc_en: Motherboards for desktop, workstation and server builds across current AMD and Intel platforms. The selection covers ATX, micro-ATX, mini-ITX and server form factors with consumer, enthusiast and workstation chipsets. Listings show socket, chipset, memory generation, PCIe layout, M.2 slot count and rear panel connectivity, so the right board can be matched to the planned CPU and case.
components/motherboards.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

The motherboard determines what the rest of the build can be. Once the socket, chipset and form factor are chosen, the CPU options, memory generation, expansion slots, storage interfaces and rear panel connectors are all fixed. Picking the wrong board often forces an upgrade that touches half the system.

What this category covers

Consumer motherboards for AMD AM5 and Intel LGA1700/1851 platforms; workstation boards with extra PCIe lanes and ECC support; and entry-level server boards in standard and proprietary form factors.

How to choose a motherboard

Start with the CPU socket and chipset

The socket sets the family of supported CPUs. The chipset on top of that decides which features are present: the number of PCIe lanes available to expansion, USB port counts, native storage support and overclocking. A more expensive chipset is not automatically better; the question is whether its features are needed.

Pick the form factor

ATX has the most expansion and the easiest layout. Micro-ATX trims one or two slots and fits smaller cases. Mini-ITX is compact but limits expansion to a single slot. Match the form factor to both the case and the planned expansion.

Plan storage and expansion before choosing

Count the M.2 NVMe drives, SATA drives, expansion cards and USB devices the build needs. The motherboard should fit them all without sharing bandwidth in ways that throttle the workload. Manuals usually note when one M.2 slot disables a SATA port or shares lanes with a PCIe slot.

Check rear panel connectivity

USB-C, USB-A counts, video outputs (for use with integrated graphics), networking type (1 GbE, 2.5 GbE, 10 GbE), Wi-Fi and Bluetooth are usually fixed on the board and cannot be expanded easily. Make sure the rear panel covers the daily peripheral needs.

Key features to compare

VRM design

The Voltage Regulator Module (VRM) feeds the CPU. Boards with stronger VRMs run high-power CPUs more reliably under sustained load. For a high-end CPU, this is worth checking in independent reviews of the specific model.

PCIe generation and lane allocation

PCIe 5.0 slots and M.2 are appearing on current platforms. Most users will not saturate them yet. The lane count to the CPU and chipset matters more, especially for builds with multiple GPUs, capture cards or high-speed storage.

BIOS update path

For new builds with the latest CPU on a platform that has been around for a while, BIOS-Flashback (a way to update the BIOS without a CPU installed) is useful. It avoids the need to find an older CPU just to bring the board up.

What to check before buying

  • Does the chosen CPU socket and chipset combination support the CPU you plan to install, on the BIOS the board ships with?
  • Does the form factor fit the case, with enough slots for the planned expansion?
  • Are there enough M.2 and SATA connections for the storage layout?
  • Does the rear panel match the peripherals (USB-C, networking speed, Wi-Fi)?
  • Will the chosen memory kit run at its rated speed on this board?

Frequently asked questions

What does the motherboard chipset actually do?

The chipset extends the CPU's input and output: extra PCIe lanes, more USB ports, SATA controllers and other connectivity. Higher-tier chipsets add features like more lanes, faster connectivity and overclocking support. Lower-tier chipsets cover the basics for everyday builds at a lower price.

Will my new CPU work on this board out of the box?

Often yes, but not always. A board sold for a long-running socket may need a BIOS update to support the latest CPU. Boards with BIOS-Flashback can be updated without a working CPU; otherwise the board may need an older CPU first to apply the update.

How many M.2 slots do I really need?
