One M.2 NVMe slot covers the operating system and primary applications. A second slot is useful for working datasets or a separate scratch volume. Beyond two, the gain depends on the workload. PCIe expansion cards can add more if needed.

Does the motherboard affect performance much?

For most workloads, two boards with the same chipset perform within a few percent of each other. The bigger differences are in features, build quality and longevity. For high-end CPUs under sustained load, the VRM design starts to matter measurably.

Do I need a server motherboard for ECC memory?

Not always. Some workstation chipsets and CPUs support ECC on consumer-style boards. Server boards add features such as remote management (BMC), more memory channels and more PCIe lanes. ECC alone does not require a server board on every platform.

components/other.meta_desc_en: Less common PC components and internal hardware that do not fit other categories: brackets, riser cables, fan controllers and small spare parts.
components/other.seo_desc_en: Internal hardware items that do not fit the main component subcategories: drive brackets and adapters, riser cables, expansion slot covers, fan controllers, RGB control hubs, mounting kits and small spare parts kept on hand for builds and repairs. The group is useful for finishing a custom build, replacing missing pieces during a repair, or stocking a workshop with the small items that often delay a job when missing.
components/other.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

This catch-all groups the smaller items that come up during builds and repairs but do not have a dedicated subcategory. Most are inexpensive, easy to overlook when planning a build, and frustrating to be without when assembly is already underway.

What this category covers

Drive brackets and 2.5-inch to 3.5-inch adapters; PCIe riser and ribbon cables; blanking plates and slot covers; fan and RGB control hubs; mounting kits for liquid cooling; replacement screws, standoffs and front-panel cables; and similar small parts.

Who this category is for

System builders finishing a build with non-standard mounting needs, repair shops keeping spare brackets and screws in stock, and IT teams maintaining a fleet that occasionally needs a missing piece without a full new case or kit.

What to look for

Compatibility with the existing build

Most items here have specific dimensions: bracket sizes, riser cable lengths, fan connector types. Confirm the measurements against the case, motherboard and components before ordering.

Quality matters more than the price suggests

A cheap riser cable with poor shielding can cause unstable PCIe operation. A flimsy bracket can flex enough to tilt a drive. For parts that affect signal integrity or mechanical stability, a known brand is usually worth the small extra cost.

Keep small spares on hand

For repair workflows, a small stock of standoffs, screws and front-panel cables prevents stalls when the original parts are missing or stripped. The cost is low compared to the time saved.

What to check before buying

  • What exact size, length or connector type does the part need to be?
  • Is the part needed for a single build or as ongoing spare stock?
  • Will it carry a high-speed signal (PCIe risers, USB extensions) where shielding matters?
  • Are the included screws, cables or fasteners enough, or are extras needed?

Frequently asked questions

Do PCIe riser cables affect performance?

A well-built shielded riser cable for the correct PCIe generation is transparent under normal use. Lower-quality cables, or risers used at PCIe 4.0 or 5.0 speeds without proper shielding, can cause the system to drop to a lower link speed for stability or to throw errors. For modern GPUs, choose risers explicitly rated for the platform's PCIe generation.

Are 2.5-inch to 3.5-inch adapters worth using for SSDs?

Yes, for cases that only have 3.5-inch HDD bays. The adapter aligns the SSD's mounting holes and screw positions with the bay. For tool-less drive cages, look for an adapter that matches the cage's pin or rail layout, not just a generic plate.

Do I need a fan controller?

Most modern motherboards have enough fan headers and good fan curve control for a typical build. A separate fan controller helps when the case has more fans than headers, when the controller offers easier physical control (knobs, displays), or when a coordinated RGB hub is required.

Can I reuse old standoffs and screws?

Standard PC builds use ISO M3 screws for drives and M3 standoffs for motherboards. Reusing them is fine if they are not stripped. Mixing different thread types (especially imperial standoffs in metric holes) can damage the case or board, so verify the type before reusing parts from older builds.

What is a slot blanking plate for?

Empty PCIe slot openings break the airflow path inside the case and let dust into the system. Blanking plates close those openings cleanly. For systems used in dusty environments, fitting them on every empty slot is worth the few minutes it takes.

components/power-protection-devices.meta_desc_en: UPS systems and PDUs for offices, racks and server rooms. Choose the right capacity, runtime, outlet count and remote management features.
components/power-protection-devices.seo_desc_en: Equipment that protects PCs, servers and network gear from power problems: Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS) for outages, sags and surges, and Power Distribution Units (PDUs) for organized power delivery in racks. Subcategories separate the two so the right type and capacity is easy to compare for a specific site or rack layout.
components/power-protection-devices.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Power problems show up as everything from a flickering monitor to a corrupted database. The two main devices in this group address different parts of the problem: a UPS keeps equipment running through outages, sags and spikes; a PDU organizes how power reaches each device in a rack and how it is monitored.

What this category covers

UPS units in offline, line-interactive and online double-conversion topologies; PDUs in basic, metered, monitored and switched configurations; rack-mount and desktop form factors; and the cables and accessories that go with them.

Who this category is for

IT teams responsible for office workstations, server rooms and small data centers; anyone running equipment that should not lose data or downtime when the mains supply hiccups.

How to choose power protection

Identify the actual risks

Short outages and noisy mains are common in many sites. A UPS handles both. Power distribution and remote outlet control in a rack are different problems and need a PDU. Many sites need both, but the right capacity for each depends on what is connected.

Size by load and runtime

For a UPS, add the running power of the connected equipment in watts (not VA), then choose a unit that can hold that load for the time it takes to either shut down cleanly or for a generator to take over. For a PDU, count the connected outlets and the per-outlet current, plus the total feed.

Decide on management features

Network-managed UPS units and switched PDUs allow remote monitoring, scheduled reboots and per-outlet control. For unattended sites, this is often essential. For a single office UPS under a desk, a simple unit with USB management is usually enough.

What to check before buying

  • What is the total wattage of the equipment to protect, including future additions?
  • How long does the runtime need to be: brief sag protection, shutdown time, or full ride-through to a generator?
  • Is remote management or alerting required?
  • Does the rack have the right depth and outlets for a metered or switched PDU?
  • Are the input and output connectors correct for the country and equipment?

Frequently asked questions

What is the difference between a UPS and a PDU?

A UPS provides backup power and conditioning so equipment stays online during outages and disturbances. A PDU distributes power from a feed to multiple outlets, often with monitoring and remote switching. The two are complementary: a UPS often feeds a PDU, which then feeds individual servers.

How long should a UPS keep my equipment running?

Long enough to ride through the typical outage, or to shut the equipment down cleanly. For office PCs, 5 to 10 minutes is usually sufficient with automatic shutdown configured. For sites with a backup generator, the UPS only has to bridge the few seconds until the generator is online.

Do I need a switched PDU or will a basic one do?

A basic PDU just distributes power. A switched PDU lets each outlet be turned on or off remotely, which is essential for unattended sites where rebooting a hung server should not require an on-site visit. Metered PDUs without switching report current draw but cannot cut individual outlets.

Will a surge strip do the job of a UPS?

No. A surge strip only handles brief spikes and does nothing for outages, sags or extended undervoltage. A UPS includes surge protection and adds battery backup with conditioning, which is what equipment with stored data actually needs.

How often should UPS batteries be replaced?

Lead-acid UPS batteries typically need replacement every three to five years; lithium-ion batteries last longer. Most managed UPS units report battery health and warn before failure. Replacing on schedule is much cheaper than discovering the failure during an outage.

components/power-protection-devices/powe.meta_desc_en: Rack PDUs in basic, metered, monitored and switched models. Compare outlet count, total current, input plug and remote management options.
components/power-protection-devices/powe.seo_desc_en: Power Distribution Units for server racks and network cabinets. The selection covers basic strip-style PDUs, metered units that report current draw, monitored units that track per-outlet figures, and switched PDUs that allow each outlet to be turned on or off remotely. Listings show outlet count, total amps, input connector and management options.
components/power-protection-devices/powe.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

A Power Distribution Unit is the rack equivalent of a power strip, with proper IEC outlets, a strong mechanical design and, in many models, monitoring or switching electronics. Choosing the right PDU is a matter of matching outlet count and current rating to the rack's load, and deciding how much remote management is needed.

What this category covers

Vertical (zero-U) and horizontal (1U) rack PDUs in basic, metered, monitored and switched configurations, with outlet counts from a few to several dozen. Different input plug types are available to match the supply at the rack.

How to choose a PDU

Pick the right tier of management

Basic PDUs only distribute power. Metered units have a local display showing total current. Monitored units report per-outlet or per-bank figures over the network. Switched units let each outlet be turned on or off remotely, which is the main feature for unattended sites and remote troubleshooting.

Match outlet count and outlet type

Count the equipment in the rack, then add headroom for future growth. Most server gear uses C13 inlets; high-current devices use C19. A PDU with a mix of both, in the right ratio, avoids forcing adapters or step-up cables.

Check the input plug and total current

The input plug must match the rack's feed: 16 A, 32 A, single-phase or three-phase, with the correct country plug. The total current rating sets the maximum sum across all outlets. Going close to the rated maximum without margin is unsafe and trips the breaker on small surges.

Consider form factor and cable management

Vertical zero-U PDUs mount on the side of the rack and free up rack units for equipment. Horizontal PDUs occupy 1U or 2U and are easier to access from the front. Cable management trays and clips reduce strain on the IEC plugs and keep airflow clean.

Key features to compare

Per-outlet metering

Per-outlet current and energy figures are useful for capacity planning and identifying problem devices. They cost more than bank-level metering but pay back in larger or growing racks.

Daisy-chaining and overall management

Some managed PDUs can share a single network connection across several units in a row. This simplifies cabling at the top of the rack and reduces the number of management ports consumed.

What to check before buying

  • Does the input plug match the rack's feed (current rating, phases, country plug)?
  • Are there enough C13 and C19 outlets, in the right mix, for current and planned equipment?
  • Is local monitoring enough, or is remote per-outlet switching required?
  • Will the PDU sit vertically (zero-U) or horizontally, and does the rack have space for that?
  • Is the management interface compatible with the existing monitoring system?

Frequently asked questions

Do I really need a managed PDU?

For a single rack with on-site staff, a basic PDU is often enough. For racks at remote sites, in shared cabinets, or in growing setups where capacity has to be tracked over time, a metered or switched PDU pays back quickly. The break-even point is usually as soon as a single avoided site visit covers the price difference.

What is a zero-U PDU?

A zero-U PDU mounts vertically on the side of the rack and does not occupy any rack units of vertical space. This frees space for servers and is the standard layout in dense racks. Horizontal PDUs occupy 1U or 2U and are still common in smaller cabinets.

Can a PDU replace a UPS?

No. A PDU only distributes the power it receives. If the input fails, every outlet on the PDU goes dark immediately. Backup power is the role of a UPS, which in many setups feeds the PDU.

How do I size the total current rating?

Add the running current of the connected equipment, then leave around 20 to 30 percent headroom for surges and future growth. Running close to the rated maximum reduces resilience and trips the breaker on normal load swings.

What is the difference between switched and outlet-switched PDUs?

Both let outlets be turned on and off remotely. Switched PDUs typically allow individual outlet control. Some lower-cost models only allow switching at the bank level, where a group of outlets shares one relay. For per-outlet reboot of specific devices, full per-outlet switching is required.

components/power-protection-devices/ups-.meta_desc_en: Uninterruptible Power Supplies in offline, line-interactive and online double-conversion topologies. Size by load, runtime and management features.
components/power-protection-devices/ups-.seo_desc_en: Uninterruptible Power Supplies for desktops, network gear and servers. The selection covers offline UPS units for basic protection, line-interactive units that handle voltage sags and surges without using the battery, and online double-conversion UPS systems that fully isolate sensitive equipment from mains disturbances. Listings show output capacity, runtime estimates, outlet count and management options so the unit matches the load.
components/power-protection-devices/ups-.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

A UPS keeps equipment running through outages, sags and spikes long enough for the system to either ride out the event or shut down cleanly. The right unit depends on the connected load, the desired runtime, and how clean the mains supply needs to be at the equipment.

What this category covers

Offline (standby) UPS units for office PCs and home networks; line-interactive units that handle voltage variation without switching to battery; online double-conversion UPS systems for servers and sensitive equipment that cannot tolerate any switchover.

How to choose a UPS

Size by real watts, not just VA

UPS units are often labelled in volt-amperes (VA) and watts. The watt figure is the meaningful one for sizing modern equipment, which has switching power supplies. Add the rated wattage of everything that will run on the UPS, then choose a unit comfortably above that, ideally with a 25 to 30 percent margin.

Decide on runtime

For automatic shutdown, 5 to 10 minutes is usually enough. For ride-through to a generator, the UPS only needs to last a few seconds. For longer outages without a generator, larger battery packs or external battery cabinets are required, and the cost rises quickly.

Choose the topology

Offline UPS units stay out of the way and switch to battery only when the mains fails. Line-interactive units regulate voltage continuously and are more tolerant of poor mains. Online double-conversion UPS systems run the load entirely from the inverter, providing a clean, stable output regardless of mains quality.

Plan management

USB or serial communication is enough for a single UPS feeding one or two computers. Network-managed UPS units allow multiple servers to monitor status and shut down in coordinated fashion. For unattended sites, network management is usually a hard requirement.

Key features to compare

Outlet count and type

Smaller UPS units use country-specific outlets; rack and server-class units use IEC C13 and C19 outlets. Pick a model with the right outlet types and enough of them for the actual equipment.

Hot-swappable batteries

For UPS units that protect critical equipment, hot-swappable battery packs let the batteries be replaced without taking the load offline. This matters far more in server rooms than in offices.

Cold start

Cold start lets the UPS power equipment from battery even when no mains is present at boot time, which is useful for portable applications and for testing.

What to check before buying

  • What is the total watt load of equipment to be protected, including future additions?
  • How long does the runtime have to be: shutdown time, ride-through to a generator, or longer?
  • Is offline, line-interactive or online double-conversion required by the equipment?
  • Are USB or network management features required for monitoring?
  • Is the form factor (tower or rack-mount) right for the install location?

Frequently asked questions

How do I know if I need an online UPS?

Online double-conversion UPS systems are needed when equipment cannot tolerate any switchover transient and when mains quality is poor or unstable. Critical servers, medical equipment and some lab gear fall into this category. For typical office PCs and most home networks, line-interactive or offline UPS units are enough.

Will my UPS protect against lightning?

UPS units include surge protection that handles typical mains spikes. A direct or near-direct lightning strike can exceed any consumer-class device's protection. For sites at high lightning risk, additional facility-level surge protection at the panel is required, in addition to the UPS.

How do I make my computer shut down automatically when the battery is low?

Connect the UPS to the computer over USB and install the manufacturer's monitoring software. Most software packages support a configurable battery threshold or remaining-time threshold at which the operating system shuts down cleanly. For multiple servers on one UPS, network management software coordinates shutdown across them.

Why do my UPS batteries fail so quickly?

Heat is the main enemy of lead-acid UPS batteries. Running a UPS in a hot equipment room halves expected battery life. Frequent deep discharges also shorten life. A UPS in a cool, ventilated location with infrequent outages routinely reaches its full battery lifespan.

Can I use a UPS with a laser printer?

Laser printers draw very high inrush current when the fuser warms up and can overload a UPS sized for office gear. Manufacturers usually recommend not connecting laser printers to a UPS, or using a separate, large unit specifically rated for printer loads.

components/power-supplies-psu.meta_desc_en: Power supplies in ATX, SFX and server form factors. Compare wattage, 80 PLUS efficiency, modular cables and rail design for builds.
components/power-supplies-psu.seo_desc_en: Power Supply Units for desktop, workstation and server builds. The selection covers ATX, SFX and SFX-L power supplies for general builds, plus dedicated server and redundant supplies. Listings show rated wattage, 80 PLUS efficiency rating, modular cable design, ATX 3.x compatibility and 12V-2x6 connector support where relevant, so the unit matches both the build's load and its future upgrade plans.
components/power-supplies-psu.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

The power supply is the part of the build that, if it fails, can take other components with it. Picking a unit with the right wattage, efficiency and quality avoids both immediate stability problems and longer-term reliability issues.

What this category covers

ATX and SFX power supplies for desktop and small form factor builds, server PSUs in 1U and 2U form factors, and redundant power supply units for servers and workstations that need continuous operation through a single PSU failure.

How to choose a power supply

Size by actual draw plus headroom

Add the rated power of the CPU and GPU under load, then add roughly 30 percent for drives, fans, motherboard and headroom. PSUs are most efficient and last longest when running at around 50 percent of their rated wattage. Going much larger than needed wastes money but is not harmful; going too small causes stability and noise problems.

Pick the right efficiency rating

80 PLUS Bronze is the entry level for office systems. Gold is the sweet spot for most builds, balancing cost and efficiency. Platinum and Titanium make sense for systems that run constantly under load, where the electricity savings add up over time.

Check the connectors

Modern high-power graphics cards may use a 12V-2x6 (the updated 12VHPWR) connector with ATX 3.x. Older cards use multiple 8-pin PCIe connectors. The PSU has to provide the right connectors natively, not through risky adapters.

Choose modular or non-modular

Fully modular PSUs let unused cables stay out of the case for cleaner airflow and easier builds. Semi-modular units leave only the essential cables fixed. Non-modular is fine for budget builds but always means more cabling to manage.

Key features to compare

Rail design

Modern PSUs are typically single-rail on the 12 V side, which simplifies high-current loads. Multi-rail designs split the 12 V into several protected groups; this is more relevant for server builds and certified industrial equipment.

Build quality and protections

Look for over-current, over-voltage, over-temperature, short-circuit and over-power protection in the specifications. Independent reviews of the specific model are the best reference for actual build quality.

Cable length

For larger cases or front-mounted PSUs, longer cables matter. Most ATX cables are sized for mid-tower cases by default; full-tower and unusual layouts may need extension cables.

What to check before buying

  • What is the actual peak power draw of the planned build?
  • Does the PSU provide the right connectors natively for the GPU?
  • Is ATX 3.x and 12V-2x6 support required for a current high-end GPU?
  • Does the form factor match the case (ATX, SFX, SFX-L)?
  • For redundant server PSUs: does the chassis support hot-swap and is the wattage matched per unit?

Frequently asked questions

How big a PSU do I really need?

For a typical office or productivity build with a mid-range CPU and integrated graphics, 450 to 550 W is comfortable. For a build with a discrete GPU, target the GPU manufacturer's recommended PSU wattage as a starting point. For high-end gaming and workstation builds with current top-tier GPUs, 850 to 1200 W is common.

Is more wattage always better?

No. A PSU that is much larger than the load runs at lower efficiency and is wasteful. A reasonable rule is to size so the typical workload runs the PSU at around 40 to 60 percent load, which is also the peak efficiency range for most units.

What does 80 PLUS Gold actually mean?

The 80 PLUS rating measures power conversion efficiency at different loads. Gold guarantees at least 87 percent efficiency at 20 percent and 100 percent load, and 90 percent at 50 percent load. Higher tiers (Platinum, Titanium) raise these thresholds. A more efficient PSU produces less heat and uses less electricity for the same workload.

Should I buy an ATX 3.x power supply?

ATX 3.x specifies behaviour for transient power spikes from modern GPUs and includes the 12V-2x6 connector. For a build with a current high-end GPU, ATX 3.x is the safer choice. For budget builds with mid-range GPUs or integrated graphics, a quality ATX 2.x PSU is fine.

How long do power supplies last?

A good-quality PSU can run for 7 to 10 years with stable performance, often longer in lighter-load office systems. Failures are most common in the first weeks of use (initial defects) or much later as capacitors age. Heat and dust shorten that lifespan; clean cases with good airflow extend it.

components/processors-cpu.meta_desc_en: Compare desktop, workstation and server processors. Find the right CPU socket, core count, clock speed and power class for your build.
components/processors-cpu.seo_desc_en: Central processing units for desktop computers, workstations and servers, organized so a system builder can compare socket type, core count, clock speed and thermal design power in one place. The category includes consumer-grade chips for general office and creative work alongside server-class processors with higher core counts, ECC support and longer-term platform stability.
components/processors-cpu.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

The processor sets the ceiling for what a build can do. Almost every other component, motherboard chipset, memory type, cooling capacity, even case size, follows from the CPU choice, which is why most builds start here.

What this category covers

Desktop processors for general productivity, workstation processors for engineering and creative workloads, and server processors built for sustained multi-threaded loads with reliability features such as Error-Correcting Code (ECC) memory support.

How to choose a processor

Start with the workload

Office work, web browsing and light creative tools run well on mid-range chips. Video editing, 3D rendering, code compilation and virtualization scale with core count. Gaming and computer-aided design lean more on single-thread speed and clock frequency. Match the chip to the dominant workload, not the headline marketing.

Pick the socket and platform

The socket, for example AM5, LGA1700, LGA1851 or SP5, determines which motherboards and memory generations the CPU is compatible with. A newer socket usually offers a longer upgrade path; an older socket may be cheaper today but reach end of life sooner.

Compare cores, threads and clock speeds

Core count and threads matter for parallel workloads. Base clock and boost clock matter for tasks that depend on a single core finishing quickly. Cache size and memory channels also affect real-world performance, especially in data-heavy work.

Plan power and cooling

The Thermal Design Power (TDP) figure on the spec sheet is a baseline; modern processors can draw significantly more under sustained load. The cooler and the case airflow have to handle the actual power draw, not just the rated TDP, otherwise the chip throttles and performance drops.

Key features to compare

Core count and architecture

More cores help with multi-threaded software, but only if the software is written to use them. Architecture generation also matters: a newer eight-core chip will often outperform an older twelve-core chip on the same workload.

Clock speed and boost behaviour

Boost clocks are usually held only on a few cores and only when temperatures allow. Sustained all-core speed under load is the more useful figure for heavy work.

Integrated graphics

An integrated GPU lets a build run without a discrete graphics card, useful for office systems and as a fallback during repairs. Server CPUs and some workstation chips skip integrated graphics and require a separate GPU.

ECC and reliability features

Server and workstation chips often support ECC memory, which corrects single-bit errors silently. For systems running calculations or databases for long periods, ECC support is worth specifying.

What to check before buying

  • Does the motherboard you have or plan to buy support this CPU socket and BIOS version?
  • Is the cooler rated for the actual power draw, not just the marketed TDP?
  • Does the case have airflow to clear that heat under sustained load?
  • Does the workload benefit more from extra cores or higher single-thread speed?
  • Will the build need ECC memory or other server-class features?
  • Is integrated graphics required as a fallback, or is a discrete GPU always present?

Frequently asked questions

Do I need a workstation CPU or will a desktop CPU do?

For most office and creative work, a high-end desktop CPU offers better value than a workstation chip. Workstation processors make sense when ECC memory, more PCIe lanes, more memory channels, or certified driver support are required by the application or by the customer.

How many cores do I actually need?

Six to eight cores cover most general work and gaming. Twelve to sixteen cores help with video editing, code compilation and light virtualization. Beyond that, the gain only appears in workloads written for heavy parallelism, such as rendering, simulation, databases and large container hosts.

Is a CPU cooler included in the box?

Lower-power consumer chips often ship with a basic cooler that handles stock workloads. Higher-power desktop chips and most workstation and server chips ship without a cooler and assume the buyer will choose one rated for the chip's actual power draw. Always confirm in the product listing.

Does a faster CPU need faster RAM?

Memory speed has a smaller effect than core count or clock speed for most workloads, but on platforms with limited memory channels, such as AM5, the gain from properly configured RAM is measurable. Check the motherboard QVL to confirm the memory kit is supported at its rated speed.

How long will a CPU last in real terms?

Hardware lifetime is usually limited by the platform reaching end of support, not by the silicon failing. A typical desktop CPU stays useful for five to seven years; a server chip may run continuously for the same period. Replace based on workload need, not age alone.

Should I buy an OEM tray CPU or a boxed retail unit?

OEM tray chips are sold without retail packaging or, often, a cooler, but the silicon is identical. They are usually intended for system integrators. Warranty terms can differ between tray and boxed parts, so check the listing if warranty length matters for the project.

components/sound-cards.meta_desc_en: Internal and external sound cards for desktops and workstations. Compare audio quality, channel support and inputs for music, voice and broadcasting.
components/sound-cards.seo_desc_en: Dedicated audio cards and external Universal Serial Bus (USB) audio interfaces for desktops, workstations and home studios. The selection covers PCIe sound cards for higher output quality, USB audio interfaces with microphone and instrument inputs, and small headphone amplifiers and digital-to-analog converters used to upgrade existing setups for music production, broadcasting and critical listening.
components/sound-cards.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Most modern motherboards include onboard audio that is good enough for office and casual use. A dedicated sound card or USB audio interface adds value when the workload involves music production, podcasting, voice work, or driving high-impedance headphones that the onboard output cannot push cleanly.

What this category covers

PCIe sound cards with higher-grade digital-to-analog converters and dedicated headphone amplifiers; USB audio interfaces with microphone preamps and instrument inputs; and small external converters and amplifiers used between a computer and analog gear.

How to choose audio hardware

Define the workload

For music production, the input chain matters: how many microphones, instruments and line inputs need to record at once, and at what sample rate. For broadcasting and voice, low input noise and a clean preamp matter most. For listening, the output chain (DAC, headphone amp) is the priority.

Internal or external

PCIe sound cards stay tied to one PC and can deliver lower latency in some setups. USB and Thunderbolt audio interfaces can move between hosts, work with notebooks, and are isolated from PC internal noise. For most current users, an external interface is the more flexible choice.

Match the input and output count

Pick an interface with a few more inputs than the current need to allow for one or two extra sources later. The number of preamp inputs is fixed at the device level; expanding usually requires an additional unit with digital input/output for chaining.

Key features to compare

Sample rate and bit depth

For most professional work, 24-bit at 48 kHz or 96 kHz is enough. Higher rates use more disk space and CPU. Higher bit depths matter more than higher sample rates for headroom during recording.

Latency and driver quality

Round-trip latency depends on the driver, buffer size and CPU. Manufacturers with mature drivers (especially on Windows ASIO and macOS Core Audio) usually outperform cheaper interfaces with the same hardware specifications.

Headphone output

For high-impedance studio headphones, a strong headphone amplifier matters more than DAC specifications. Listings usually note the maximum supported impedance and output power.

What to check before buying

  • How many simultaneous inputs and outputs does the workflow need?
  • Is the device meant for one fixed PC, or should it move between hosts?
  • Does the headphone amplifier match the impedance of the planned headphones?
  • Are the drivers stable on the operating systems in use?

Frequently asked questions

Do I still need a sound card?

For office, video calls and casual listening, onboard audio is usually fine. A dedicated card or USB interface helps when the workload involves recording, broadcasting, mixing, or driving high-end headphones. For most general builds, the onboard output is enough.

What is the difference between a DAC and a sound card?

A digital-to-analog converter (DAC) converts a digital audio stream to an analog signal. A sound card includes a DAC and usually an analog-to-digital converter (ADC) for inputs, plus extras like preamps, headphone amplifiers and digital outputs. A standalone DAC focuses only on output quality.

USB or PCIe audio interface?

USB and Thunderbolt interfaces are more flexible: they move between hosts, work with notebooks, and avoid PC internal noise. PCIe cards can have lower latency in some setups but are tied to one PC. For most users today, USB is the practical choice.

How important is the microphone preamp?

For voice and instrument recording, the preamp is the part of the chain that sets the noise floor. A clean preamp on a modest interface usually outperforms a noisy preamp on a more expensive unit. Independent reviews of the specific model are the best reference.

Does sample rate above 96 kHz matter for general use?

For most listening and even most production work, no. Higher sample rates use more storage and CPU and rarely produce an audible difference. They mainly matter in specific scientific, archival or specialized audio workflows.

components/storage-2.meta_desc_en: Storage drives for desktops, notebooks and servers. Compare SSDs, HDDs, USB flash, memory cards and external drives by capacity and speed.
components/storage-2.seo_desc_en: Storage devices for desktop, notebook, workstation and server use: internal Solid State Drives (SSDs), Hard Disk Drives (HDDs), external drives, USB flash sticks and memory cards with their readers. Subcategories separate the main media types so capacity, interface and form factor are compared within the right group rather than across very different products.
components/storage-2.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Storage decisions affect speed, capacity, durability and price in different proportions depending on the medium. Modern builds usually mix a fast Solid State Drive for the operating system and active data with larger Hard Disk Drives or external storage for archives and backups.

What this category covers

Internal SSDs in M.2 NVMe and 2.5-inch SATA form factors, internal and external HDDs, portable SSDs and HDDs, USB flash sticks, memory cards and the readers used with them. Subcategories sort by medium so the right options stay together.

Who this category is for

System builders, IT teams provisioning fleets, content creators who manage large datasets, and anyone replacing a failing drive or expanding the storage capacity of an existing system.

How to plan storage

Think in tiers

A typical layout is: a fast SSD for the operating system and active applications, a larger SSD or HDD for working datasets, and external storage or network storage for backups and archives. Each tier can be sized and budgeted separately, which usually gives better value than buying one very large fast drive.

Match the interface to the workload

NVMe SSDs reach speeds that matter for video editing, large file copies and database work. SATA SSDs are still excellent for everyday use and far faster than HDDs. HDDs remain the right choice for bulk storage at low cost per gigabyte.

Plan capacity with margin

Drives slow down and wear faster when filled close to capacity. Aim to keep free space at 15 to 20 percent or more on SSDs, and on HDDs to leave room for fragmentation and future growth. Buying one tier larger than the immediate need is often cheaper than upgrading later.

What to check before buying

  • What is the right medium for this tier (NVMe SSD, SATA SSD, HDD, external)?
  • Does the host have the right interface (M.2 slot generation, SATA port count, USB version)?
  • Is the capacity sized with margin for the next year or two of growth?
  • Is endurance (TBW or DWPD) a concern for the workload?
  • Is a backup or redundancy plan in place beyond the primary drive?

Frequently asked questions

How much faster is NVMe than SATA SSD in everyday use?

For boot times, application launches and small file work, the difference between a SATA SSD and an NVMe SSD is small. For large file copies, video editing and database workloads, NVMe is several times faster. The price difference between the two has narrowed enough that NVMe is the default choice on supported systems.

Are HDDs still worth buying?

Yes, for bulk storage. The cost per gigabyte for HDDs remains much lower than for SSDs above a certain capacity. For backups, archives, video libraries and any workload that does not need fast random access, HDDs are still the practical choice.

What is the difference between consumer and enterprise drives?

Enterprise drives are rated for 24/7 operation, higher write workloads, and longer warranty periods. They cost more but pay back in environments with sustained, mixed read and write loads. Consumer drives are tuned for typical desktop use and shorter active hours.

Do I still need to back up an SSD?

Absolutely. SSDs do not fail mechanically the way HDDs can, but they fail electronically, often suddenly. The reliability of any single drive is no substitute for a backup. The 3-2-1 rule (three copies, on two media, one off-site) still applies regardless of media type.

Will a faster SSD make my old computer faster?

Replacing an HDD with any SSD is the single biggest perceived speed upgrade for an older computer. Replacing a SATA SSD with NVMe in an old computer brings smaller gains, since the bottleneck is usually elsewhere. The largest improvement comes from the first SSD upgrade.

components/storage-2/flash.meta_desc_en: USB flash drives in USB-A, USB-C and dual-connector designs. Compare capacity, read and write speeds, and form factors for daily use.
components/storage-2/flash.seo_desc_en: USB flash drives for transferring files, carrying installation media and short-term storage. The selection covers traditional USB-A sticks, USB-C sticks for current notebooks, dual-connector models with both interfaces, and small form factor drives that stay plugged in. Listings show capacity, read and write speeds, the supported USB generation, and any rugged or encrypted features.
components/storage-2/flash.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

USB flash drives are the simplest portable storage: small, durable, and useful for transferring files, carrying installation images and keeping a backup copy of important documents. The market spans cheap promotional drives to fast premium models that approach external SSD speeds.

What this category covers

Standard USB-A sticks, USB-C sticks for current notebooks and tablets, dual-connector drives with both A and C, and low-profile drives that fit flush with a USB port for permanent use.

How to choose a USB flash drive

Match the connector to the host

USB-A is still on most desktops and many notebooks. USB-C is increasingly common on newer machines. A drive with both connectors avoids carrying a separate adapter and works with desktops, notebooks and most modern tablets.

Pick the USB generation by use case

USB 2.0 sticks are slow (up to 30 MB/s in practice) and are only suitable for very small files or non-time-sensitive use. USB 3.x drives are several times faster and the right choice for any meaningful amount of data, particularly large media files or backup copies.

Read and write speeds matter differently

Marketing usually highlights read speed. Write speed is what determines how fast files actually copy onto the drive, and it is often much slower than read speed on cheaper drives. For frequent writing, look up the rated write speed, not just the read figure.

Consider durability for travel

Drives with metal bodies, rubberized casings or pull-back connectors survive keychains and bags better than capped plastic sticks. Lost caps are still the most common failure mode for cheap flash drives.

Key features to compare

Capacity

Common capacities run from 16 GB to 1 TB and beyond. For installation media and document transfer, 32 to 64 GB is usually enough. For carrying media files or large backups, 256 GB and above are now affordable.

Encryption

Some drives include hardware encryption with a PIN or fingerprint. For carrying sensitive documents, this is meaningful protection if the drive is lost. Software encryption (BitLocker To Go, VeraCrypt) on a regular drive achieves a similar result.

What to check before buying

  • Which connector does the host actually have, and is a dual-connector design useful?
  • What is the rated write speed (not just the marketed read speed)?
  • Will the drive be plugged in permanently, or carried and unplugged often?
  • Is hardware encryption needed for the data being carried?

Frequently asked questions

Why is my USB flash drive slower than advertised?

Many cheap drives quote burst read speeds, with much lower sustained write speeds. The host port version, the cable on USB-C drives, and the file system also affect the result. For an honest comparison, look at sustained write speeds in independent reviews.

Are USB flash drives reliable enough for backups?

Flash drives can fail without warning, often electronically or because the connector wears. They are fine as a transfer or short-term backup medium but not a primary backup. For backups that matter, use a proper external drive or a network backup, with a second copy off-site.

What file system should I use on a USB flash drive?

For drives larger than 32 GB used across Windows, macOS and Linux, exFAT is the most compatible choice. NTFS is best for Windows-only use with files larger than 4 GB. FAT32 is universally readable but caps individual file size at 4 GB.

How long does data last on a flash drive?

Data on a flash drive that sits unused can become unreadable after several years. The exact retention depends on the flash type and storage temperature. Drives in regular use refresh themselves and stay readable much longer; drives left in a drawer for archival storage are not a safe long-term solution.

Do USB flash drives work on tablets and phones?

USB-C flash drives work directly with most current tablets and Android phones. iPhones with USB-C ports also support standard storage drives. Older devices with Lightning ports need a specific Lightning-compatible drive or an adapter.

components/storage-2/hdds.meta_desc_en: Internal and external hard drives for desktops, NAS and backups. Compare capacity, RPM, cache and SATA or USB interface for the right fit.
components/storage-2/hdds.seo_desc_en: Hard Disk Drives for bulk storage, backups, archives and Network Attached Storage (NAS) use. The selection covers internal 3.5-inch and 2.5-inch drives in different RPM and workload classes, alongside external desktop and portable hard drives. Subcategories separate internal from external so the form factor and interface match the use, and so workload class can be compared within a single tier.
components/storage-2/hdds.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Hard drives remain the most cost-effective way to store large amounts of data. They are slower than Solid State Drives but make sense for backups, media libraries, archives and any workload where capacity matters more than speed. The category covers both internal drives that mount in a PC or NAS, and external drives that connect over USB.

What this category covers

Internal 3.5-inch desktop and NAS hard drives, internal 2.5-inch drives for notebooks and small chassis, external desktop drives with their own power supply, and portable USB hard drives that are bus-powered.

Who this category is for

System builders adding bulk storage, IT teams running backup or archive systems, photographers and video editors holding large media libraries, and anyone replacing a failing drive in a notebook or desktop.

How to choose a hard drive

Internal or external

Internal drives sit inside the PC or NAS, are usually faster, and use the SATA interface. External drives are easier to add and move between machines but depend on the USB connection for performance. For permanent capacity expansion, internal is usually the right choice; for portable backups and offsite copies, external.

Match the workload class

Drives are sold in workload tiers: desktop drives for typical PC use, NAS drives rated for 24/7 multi-bay operation, surveillance drives optimized for continuous video write, and enterprise drives with the longest warranties and highest workload ratings. Using a desktop drive in a NAS or surveillance setup shortens its life noticeably.

Plan capacity with redundancy in mind

For NAS and other multi-drive setups, a single drive failure should not cause data loss. RAID 1, RAID 5 or RAID 6 reduce usable capacity but protect against drive failure. Buying a slightly smaller capacity per drive in a redundant array is usually safer than a single very large drive.

What to check before buying

  • Is the drive intended for desktop, NAS, surveillance or enterprise workloads?
  • Does the host have the right interface and form factor (SATA 3.5-inch, SATA 2.5-inch, USB external)?
  • Is the RPM and cache size matched to the speed expectations?
  • Is there a backup or redundancy plan beyond a single drive?
  • For external drives, is the USB version on the host fast enough to use the drive's speed?

Frequently asked questions

How long does a hard drive last?

Typical hard drive lifespan is three to five years of active use, with NAS and enterprise drives usually lasting longer. Drives often run for many more years before failure, but planning around the typical figure with regular backups is the safer approach. Drives that have failed once should not be reused for important data.

Can I use a desktop drive in a NAS?

Mechanically yes, but the warranty and reliability assumptions differ. Desktop drives are rated for around eight hours of daily use; NAS drives are rated for 24/7 use with vibration tolerance for multi-drive bays. Using a desktop drive in a NAS shortens its lifespan and may invalidate the warranty.

Should I buy a Shingled Magnetic Recording (SMR) drive?

SMR drives are slower at sustained writes than Conventional Magnetic Recording (CMR) drives and can interact badly with RAID rebuilds. For typical archival use, SMR is fine and the price is lower. For NAS, RAID and any write-heavy workload, CMR is the safer choice.

What is the difference between desktop and external hard drives?

The drive inside an external desktop hard drive is often the same as a separately sold internal drive, packaged with a USB bridge and a power supply. The external form factor is more convenient for casual use; internal mounting is faster, has fewer points of failure, and is usually cheaper per gigabyte.

Why is my external hard drive slow?

The most common causes are an underpowered USB port, a USB cable rated only for USB 2.0 speeds, or the drive itself running on a 5400 RPM mechanism. Try a different USB port and a known good cable, then check the rated drive speed. For SSD-like speed, an external SSD is required.

components/storage-2/hdds/external.meta_desc_en: Desktop and portable external hard drives for backups, archives and media. Compare capacity, USB version and bus-powered or mains-powered design.
components/storage-2/hdds/external.seo_desc_en: External Hard Disk Drives for backups, file archives and media libraries. The selection covers larger desktop drives with their own power supplies and capacities up to 24 TB, and smaller portable drives that draw power from the USB port. Listings show capacity, USB version, included cable and any additional software bundled with the drive.
components/storage-2/hdds/external.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

External hard drives add storage to a PC or notebook in minutes, with no internal installation. They are the simplest way to keep a backup, hold a media library that does not have to live on the main drive, or move large amounts of data between machines.

What this category covers

Desktop external HDDs, which are larger units with their own power supply and the highest available capacities; portable external HDDs in 2.5-inch form factor, which are bus-powered and travel easily; and ruggedized portable drives with reinforced enclosures for fieldwork.

How to choose an external hard drive

Desktop or portable

Desktop external drives offer higher capacities and are the right choice for fixed backup and archive use. Portable drives are smaller, draw power from the USB port, and travel without an extra power brick. The trade-off is capacity: portable drives top out lower, and the largest portable drives are slower than equivalent desktop drives.

Plan around the USB connection

The drive's interface limits its speed to that of the host port. A USB 3.x desktop drive on a USB 2.0 port runs at USB 2.0 speed. Verify that the most-used host has the right USB version and that the supplied cable matches.

Backups need more than a single drive

An external drive used as the only backup is one drop or one failure away from data loss. For backups that matter, keep at least two copies on different drives, and keep one of them off-site. The 3-2-1 rule applies to external drives as much as to any other backup medium.

Key features to compare

Capacity per drive

Portable external HDDs run up to about 5 TB. Desktop external HDDs go well beyond, with 12 to 24 TB models common. Higher capacities cost less per gigabyte but mean more data on a single point of failure.

Drive speed and cache

Most portable HDDs run at 5400 RPM. Desktop drives may run at 5400 or 7200 RPM with larger cache. For backup workloads, the difference matters mainly during the initial full backup; after that, incremental backups are usually small enough that drive speed is not the bottleneck.

Software and encryption

Some drives come with backup software or hardware encryption. Many users choose to ignore the bundled software and use a backup tool of their choice or the operating system's built-in tools. Hardware encryption is a useful feature for portable drives carrying sensitive data.

What to check before buying

  • Will the drive be fixed in one place or carried frequently?
  • Is the capacity enough for both the current data and the next year of growth?
  • Does the host PC have a USB port that matches the drive's rated speed?
  • Is the drive part of a single backup, or one of several copies in a proper backup plan?
  • Does the data being stored need hardware encryption?

Frequently asked questions

Is an external SSD better than an external HDD?

For speed, durability and noise, external SSDs are clearly better. For capacity at low cost, external HDDs still win above a few terabytes. The right choice depends on the use: SSDs for active working data and travel, HDDs for backups and archives where speed is less important.

Why does my external drive disconnect when I plug in another USB device?

This is usually a power issue on bus-powered drives. The host USB port cannot supply enough current when several devices share it. Move the drive to its own port directly on the host, or use a powered USB hub.

Can I leave a desktop external HDD running constantly?

Most desktop external HDDs are designed for intermittent use. Leaving one running 24/7 is possible but shortens the drive's expected life and is harder on the bridge electronics in the enclosure. For continuous use, a NAS with proper drives is a better long-term solution.

Should I format an external HDD before first use?

Most drives ship pre-formatted in exFAT or NTFS and work out of the box. For a clean start, especially when moving between Windows and macOS, reformatting to the desired file system is straightforward and only takes a few minutes for a quick format.

How do I dispose of an old external drive securely?

For drives that held sensitive data, a full overwrite or a manufacturer's secure erase is the minimum step before disposal. For drives that will not be reused, physical destruction (drilling through the platters in a HDD) is the most reliable approach. Encrypted drives are easier to retire safely because deleting the encryption key effectively renders the data unreadable.

components/storage-2/hdds/internal.meta_desc_en: Internal HDDs in 3.5-inch and 2.5-inch SATA form factors. Compare capacity, RPM, cache and workload class for desktops, NAS and surveillance.
components/storage-2/hdds/internal.seo_desc_en: Internal Hard Disk Drives in 3.5-inch and 2.5-inch SATA form factors for desktops, Network Attached Storage (NAS), surveillance recorders and enterprise servers. Listings show capacity, rotational speed, cache size, recording technology and the rated workload tier so the right drive is matched to the actual install, not just to the headline cost-per-gigabyte figure.
components/storage-2/hdds/internal.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Internal Hard Disk Drives remain the most cost-effective way to add bulk storage to a desktop or NAS. The mechanical design and rated workload class still matter, even after years of refinement. Picking the right tier extends drive life and avoids data loss in multi-drive systems.

What this category covers

3.5-inch SATA HDDs in desktop, NAS, surveillance and enterprise tiers, and 2.5-inch SATA HDDs for notebooks and small chassis. Capacities run from a few hundred gigabytes to over 20 TB depending on the tier.

How to choose an internal hard drive

Pick the workload tier

Desktop drives are tuned for typical eight-hour-day office and home use. NAS drives are rated for 24/7 multi-bay operation with vibration tolerance. Surveillance drives are tuned for continuous video write workloads. Enterprise drives have the longest warranties and highest workload ratings, with corresponding price.

Confirm Conventional or Shingled recording

Conventional Magnetic Recording (CMR) drives perform consistently and rebuild quickly in RAID. Shingled Magnetic Recording (SMR) drives are slower at sustained writes and can interact badly with RAID parity rebuilds. For NAS and RAID, choose CMR. For pure archive use, SMR is acceptable and cheaper.

Match RPM and cache to expectations

5400 RPM drives are quieter, run cooler and use less power. 7200 RPM drives are faster, particularly for sequential reads. Cache size (typically 64 to 512 MB) matters for short bursts of mixed work. None of this changes the basic order of magnitude difference between any HDD and any SSD.

Plan for redundancy

Single-drive setups should always have backups. Multi-drive setups should use a RAID level appropriate to the data: RAID 1 for two-drive setups where redundancy is the priority, RAID 5 for 4 to 5 bays, RAID 6 for larger arrays where two-drive failures must be survivable.

Key features to compare

Capacity per drive

Modern 3.5-inch HDDs reach 22 TB and beyond. The largest drives have the lowest cost per gigabyte but lengthen RAID rebuild times significantly, which raises the risk window during a failure. For larger arrays, slightly smaller drives are sometimes the safer choice.

Workload rating in TB written per year

Drive specifications now include an annual workload rating. Desktop drives are usually rated for 55 TB per year; NAS drives for 180 TB; enterprise drives for 550 TB or more. Match the rating to the expected use.

What to check before buying

  • Is the drive a CMR or SMR design, and does the use case demand CMR?
  • Is the workload tier matched to the install (desktop, NAS, surveillance, enterprise)?
  • Does the host have the right SATA port count and the cabling for it?
  • For RAID arrays, is a hot spare or a same-tier replacement available?
  • Is a backup plan in place beyond the single drive or array?

Frequently asked questions

Are 7200 RPM drives much faster than 5400 RPM drives?

For sequential read and write, 7200 RPM drives are noticeably faster, often by 30 to 40 percent. For random access, the difference is smaller. For most office and backup workloads, either speed is fine. The decisive performance difference is between any HDD and any SSD, not between HDD speeds.

Why are NAS drives more expensive than desktop drives of the same capacity?

NAS drives are rated for 24/7 operation, include vibration tolerance for multi-drive bays, and ship with longer warranties and higher workload ratings. The price difference reflects the longer expected life and the support for multi-drive use, not better raw performance.

Should I buy a Helium-filled HDD?

Helium-filled drives use less power, run cooler and reach higher capacities by allowing more platters in the same case height. They cost slightly more but are now the standard at the highest capacities. For typical NAS and enterprise use, helium drives are a sensible default.

Can I mix drive sizes in a RAID array?

Most RAID levels treat the array as bound by the smallest drive. A larger drive in a mixed array contributes only the size of the smallest drive, with the rest unused. For best capacity utilization, all drives in an array should be the same size and ideally the same model.

How do I move data from an old HDD to a new one?

Use a disk cloning tool to image the old drive to the new one, or back up and restore the data. For boot drives, cloning is faster but requires the new drive to be at least as large as the data on the old one. For arrays, the RAID controller's expansion or replacement function handles the move drive by drive.

components/storage-2/memory-cards-reader.meta_desc_en: SD, microSD, CFexpress and CompactFlash memory cards plus matching readers. Compare capacity, speed class and host compatibility.
components/storage-2/memory-cards-reader.seo_desc_en: Memory cards for cameras, drones, action cameras, dashcams, handheld game consoles and notebooks, plus the readers used to copy data from them onto a PC. The selection covers SD and microSD cards in different speed classes, CFexpress and CompactFlash cards for professional cameras, and single-format and multi-format readers that match the host port.
components/storage-2/memory-cards-reader.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Memory cards are the small removable storage used in cameras, drones, dashcams, handheld consoles and many specialized devices. The card's speed class and the host's compatibility decide whether the device records cleanly without dropping frames or corrupting files.

What this category covers

Standard SD cards and high-capacity SDXC cards for cameras and notebooks; microSD cards for action cameras, drones and phones; CFexpress Type A and Type B cards for current professional cameras; CompactFlash cards for older equipment; and the readers that go with each format.

How to choose a memory card

Match the speed class to the workload

SD cards carry several overlapping ratings. UHS speed class (U1, U3) and Video Speed Class (V30, V60, V90) are what matter for video. A V30 card handles 4K at typical bitrates; V60 and V90 are required for high-bitrate 4K and 6K or 8K recording. Photo bursts also benefit from higher speed classes.

Check device compatibility

Cameras list both the supported card type and the maximum capacity. An SDXC card may not work in older equipment limited to SDHC. CFexpress Type A and Type B are not interchangeable. The device manual is the authoritative reference.

Capacity is one of several factors

For cameras, capacity should fit a typical day of shooting plus margin, with multiple smaller cards often being safer than one large card. For dashcams and continuous-record devices, very large cards are useful but the speed class and endurance rating matter more.

Endurance matters for continuous-record use

Standard memory cards are not designed for the continuous overwrite cycles of dashcams and surveillance. High-endurance variants are sold specifically for these uses and last much longer in the same setting.

Key features to compare

UHS-I versus UHS-II

UHS-II SD cards have an extra row of pins and higher speeds but only at full performance in UHS-II hosts and readers. In a UHS-I device, a UHS-II card runs at UHS-I speed.

Card type and speed alignment

A fast card in a slow host is wasted; a slow card in a fast host is the bottleneck. The slowest part of card, host and reader sets the actual transfer rate.

What to check before buying

  • Which exact card format and capacity does the device support?
  • What speed class is required for the recording mode (resolution and frame rate)?
  • Will the card be used in continuous-record applications that need a high-endurance variant?
  • Does the workflow include a fast reader to match the card's rated speed?

Frequently asked questions

What does V30 mean on an SD card?

V30 is the Video Speed Class designation. It guarantees a minimum sustained write speed of 30 MB/s, which is the requirement for most consumer 4K video. V60 and V90 cards guarantee 60 MB/s and 90 MB/s respectively, needed for higher-bitrate professional video.

Is a faster card better even for slower devices?

A faster card will work in a slower device, but the device caps the actual write speed at its own maximum. The benefit appears later, when copying files off the card on a fast reader, or if the card moves to a faster device.

Why do my cards fail more often than expected?

Common causes are removing the card while the device is still writing, using a card not rated for the workload (especially in continuous-record dashcams), counterfeit cards from unauthorized sellers, or simply heavy use over years. Buy from authorized resellers and match the endurance rating to the use.

How do I tell genuine cards from counterfeits?

Counterfeit cards often look identical to genuine ones but show false capacity or fail under sustained load. Buy from authorized retailers, check the card's actual capacity with a verification tool such as F3 or H2testw on first use, and avoid suspiciously low prices for high capacities.

What is the difference between SD, SDHC and SDXC?

The three are generations of the SD standard. SD covers up to 2 GB, SDHC up to 32 GB, SDXC up to 2 TB. Newer devices read all three; older devices may only read up to SDHC. The card format used at the time of the device's release is usually a safe minimum.

components/storage-2/other.meta_desc_en: Less common storage devices: external optical drives, tape drives, optical media and specialty storage hardware for backups and archives.
components/storage-2/other.seo_desc_en: Storage hardware that does not fit the main media subcategories: external Universal Serial Bus (USB) optical drives for CD, DVD and Blu-ray, tape drives and tape cartridges used for long-term backup, and specialty storage devices kept on hand for migration, archival and rare access workflows where current SSDs and HDDs cannot serve the original media.
components/storage-2/other.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

This catch-all groups storage devices that are no longer the main daily option but remain important for specific tasks: reading older media, performing long-term archival backups, or interfacing with industrial and specialized equipment that still uses optical or tape storage.

What this category covers

External USB CD, DVD and Blu-ray drives for notebooks and modern desktops without internal optical bays; LTO tape drives and tape cartridges for long-term backup; blank optical media; and specialty storage adapters for legacy interfaces.

Who this category is for

IT departments running long-term backup and compliance archives on tape; users who need to read or burn legacy CDs, DVDs and Blu-ray discs; anyone working with older equipment or media that cannot move to current storage standards.

What to look for

Optical drives

External USB optical drives are the simplest option for reading legacy discs. Most are bus-powered and work without drivers on current operating systems. Blu-ray drives also read DVDs and CDs and are useful where archival Blu-ray discs are still in use.

Tape backup

LTO tape remains the lowest cost-per-gigabyte option for offline backup at scale and is the standard medium for long-term retention. The current LTO generation determines compatibility with cartridges of different ages: drives can usually read two generations back and write one generation back.

Specialty media

Some industrial equipment still uses older media (floppy drives, magneto-optical disks, ZIP cartridges). Adapter drives for these formats are still produced in small numbers and matter for organizations with legacy gear.

What to check before buying

  • What media will actually need to be read or written, and is it CD, DVD, Blu-ray, tape or something legacy?
  • For tape: which LTO generation matches the existing cartridges or backup software?
  • Is the drive a one-time use (data migration) or a permanent part of the workflow?
  • Does the operating system support the drive without specialized drivers?

Frequently asked questions

Are optical drives still useful?

For most users, no. Software distribution moved online and storage moved to USB drives. Optical drives still matter for reading legacy archives, working with installation discs for older equipment, and some niche broadcast and audio workflows where physical media is still in use.

Is tape backup still relevant?

For backup at scale, yes. LTO tape has the lowest cost per gigabyte for offline storage, very long shelf life when stored properly, and is fully offline (immune to ransomware that encrypts online storage). For long-term retention and compliance archives, tape is still the standard.

How long does data last on optical media?

Standard CD-R and DVD-R media degrade over years to decades depending on quality and storage conditions. Archival-grade media (M-DISC) is rated for much longer retention. For long-term archives, optical is risky compared to tape unless archival-grade media and proper storage are used.

Can I still read floppy disks?

Yes. USB floppy drives are still produced for users who need to read 3.5-inch disks. Older 5.25-inch disks need specialty drives that are now mostly second-hand. The bigger problem is often the format on the disks rather than reading them physically.

Why does my external optical drive eject discs?

Bus-powered USB optical drives are sensitive to underpowered USB ports. Try a different port directly on the host, or use the secondary power cable some drives include. Old or damaged discs that the drive cannot read also trigger an eject.

components/storage-2/ssd.meta_desc_en: Internal and external SSDs in NVMe and SATA form factors. Compare capacity, sequential and random speeds, endurance and connectivity.
components/storage-2/ssd.seo_desc_en: Solid State Drives (SSDs) for desktop, notebook and server installations, and external portable SSDs for travel and backup. The selection covers Non-Volatile Memory Express (NVMe) drives in M.2 and U.2 form factors, SATA SSDs in 2.5-inch form factor, and external Universal Serial Bus and Thunderbolt drives. Subcategories separate internal from external.
components/storage-2/ssd.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

SSDs are the default storage for most modern systems. They have no moving parts, use less power than HDDs, and are much faster across both sequential and random workloads. Within the category, the form factor and interface decide which platforms a given drive can be installed in.

What this category covers

Internal NVMe SSDs in M.2 2280 form factor (the most common), high-capacity U.2 NVMe drives for servers and workstations, 2.5-inch SATA SSDs for upgrade and notebook use, and external portable SSDs over USB and Thunderbolt.

Who this category is for

System builders specifying boot and primary drives, IT teams provisioning fleet upgrades, content creators managing fast working datasets, and anyone replacing a slower HDD or older SSD with current storage.

How to plan SSD storage

Internal or external

Internal SSDs are faster, cheaper per gigabyte, and the default for system drives. External SSDs are flexible, easy to move between hosts, and protect data in transit. For working datasets that move with a notebook, external SSDs are the right choice; for system drives, internal.

NVMe or SATA

NVMe drives connect over PCIe and reach speeds several times higher than SATA SSDs. For new builds on platforms with M.2 slots, NVMe is the default. SATA SSDs are still the right upgrade path for older PCs and notebooks without M.2 support, and are usually cheaper per gigabyte.

Match endurance to write workload

Drive endurance is rated in Terabytes Written (TBW) or Drive Writes Per Day (DWPD). Consumer drives are sized for typical desktop use; data center drives are rated for much heavier sustained write workloads. For databases and write-heavy server workloads, choose data center drives even if the capacity figures look the same.

What to check before buying

  • Is the form factor right for the host (M.2 NVMe slot generation, 2.5-inch SATA bay, U.2 backplane)?
  • Does the workload need NVMe speed or is SATA enough?
  • For NVMe: is the slot PCIe 3.0, 4.0 or 5.0, and does the drive's rated speed match it?
  • Is the endurance rating sized for the actual write workload?
  • Is a heatsink required for high-speed PCIe 4.0 or 5.0 NVMe drives?

Frequently asked questions

How long do SSDs last?

Modern consumer SSDs typically last well beyond their warranty in normal use. Endurance is bounded by total bytes written; for a typical office workload, that limit is rarely reached. Server and database workloads, with heavier sustained writing, benefit from data center drives with much higher TBW figures.

Do SSDs slow down as they fill up?

Yes, especially above 80 percent full. Free space gives the drive's controller room to manage wear and write efficiently. Keeping at least 15 to 20 percent free, or using a drive larger than strictly needed, keeps speed and endurance steady over time.

Will I notice the difference between PCIe 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0 NVMe?

For everyday use, no. Booting, application launches and small file work look almost identical between generations. The difference appears in large file copies, video editing of high-bitrate footage, and data processing of large datasets, where the higher generations are clearly faster.

Should I worry about the heat from a fast NVMe drive?

Yes for high-end PCIe 4.0 and especially PCIe 5.0 drives under sustained load. Without a heatsink, the drive will throttle to protect itself. Many motherboards now include built-in M.2 heatsinks; aftermarket heatsinks are inexpensive and effective for cases that lack them.

Can I use an external SSD as a system drive?

For some operating systems and use cases, yes, but the connection (USB or Thunderbolt) becomes a single point of failure and can disconnect mid-use. For a permanent system drive, internal storage is more reliable. External SSDs work well for portable installations, repair drives and live operating systems.

components/storage-2/ssd/external.meta_desc_en: Portable external SSDs over USB-C and Thunderbolt. Compare capacity, sustained speed and rugged designs for travel, editing and backups.
components/storage-2/ssd/external.seo_desc_en: Portable external Solid State Drives for fast file transfer, video editing on location, and travel-ready backup. The selection covers USB 3.x drives that run over USB-C and USB-A, faster USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 drives, and Thunderbolt SSDs that approach internal NVMe speeds. Listings show capacity, rated speed, host requirements and any rugged or weather-rated design features.
components/storage-2/ssd/external.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

External SSDs combine fast, durable storage with a small form factor and a single cable to the host. They have replaced external HDDs as the default portable storage for users who value speed, durability and silent operation, especially in video, design and travel use cases.

What this category covers

USB 3.x external SSDs that work with most current desktops and notebooks; faster USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 drives for hosts that support 20 Gbps; and Thunderbolt 3 and Thunderbolt 4 SSDs that approach internal NVMe speeds when paired with a Thunderbolt host.

How to choose an external SSD

Match the interface to the host

The drive's rated speed only applies if the host port supports the same interface. A 20 Gbps drive on a regular USB 3.x 10 Gbps port runs at 10 Gbps. A Thunderbolt drive on a regular USB-C port may not work at all or runs at a much lower rate. Verify the host port type before ordering.

Look at sustained write speed

Most external SSDs quote a peak read speed. Sustained write speed during long transfers is what matters in real workflows like video editing or backup. Some drives slow down dramatically once a small fast cache is full. Independent reviews are the best reference for sustained behaviour.

Consider build and durability

Drives meant for travel or fieldwork have rubberized cases, dust and water ratings (IP65 and similar), and shock tolerance figures. For office and travel between meetings, a basic well-built drive is enough. For active outdoor or industrial use, choose a rugged model.

Plan capacity with margin

External SSDs are commonly used for working data that grows over time. Buying one tier larger than the immediate need is often cheaper than replacing the drive a year later. SSDs also slow down when very full, so leaving headroom helps.

Key features to compare

Connector type and included cable

USB-C is the modern standard. Some drives include a USB-C to USB-A cable for compatibility with older hosts. Thunderbolt drives need a Thunderbolt-rated cable, not a generic USB-C one.

Hardware encryption

Some external SSDs include hardware-based AES encryption with a PIN entered through software or a small keypad on the drive. For carrying sensitive data, this adds meaningful protection without depending on the operating system.

Power draw

Most external SSDs are bus-powered and work without an external supply. On weak or shared USB ports, the drive may disconnect under load. Plugging directly into a host port or a powered USB hub usually fixes this.

What to check before buying

  • Does the host actually support the drive's rated interface (USB 3.x, USB 3.2 Gen 2x2, Thunderbolt)?
  • Is the rated sustained write speed enough for the typical workload?
  • Will the drive be carried in a bag and need a rugged design?
  • Does the data require hardware encryption?
  • Is the included cable the right one for the most-used host port?

Frequently asked questions

Why is my external SSD slower than advertised?

The host port version is the most common cause. A 20 Gbps drive on a 10 Gbps port runs at 10 Gbps; a USB 3.x drive on a USB 2.0 port runs at 30 to 40 MB/s. The cable also matters: short, certified cables are more reliable at high speeds than long generic ones.

Can I use an external SSD for video editing?

Yes, particularly with a USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 or Thunderbolt drive. For 1080p and standard 4K editing, even USB 3.x drives work fine. For multicam or high-bitrate 4K and 8K editing, Thunderbolt SSDs are usually the right choice.

Are external SSDs better than external HDDs for backups?

For speed, durability and noise, yes. For capacity at low cost, external HDDs still win above a few terabytes. The right choice depends on the priority: SSDs for active backup of important data, HDDs for large infrequent archival backups.

How do I securely erase an external SSD?

For drives with hardware encryption, deleting the encryption key (often called secure erase or crypto-erase) renders the data unreadable instantly. For drives without hardware encryption, the manufacturer's tool or the operating system's secure erase function overwrites the drive. Standard delete commands do not securely erase SSDs.

Does an external SSD work the same on Windows and macOS?

Hardware-wise, yes. The factory file system is often exFAT for cross-platform compatibility. For best performance on a single platform, reformatting to that platform's native file system (NTFS for Windows, APFS for macOS) is straightforward.

components/storage-2/ssd/internal.meta_desc_en: Internal SSDs in M.2 NVMe and 2.5-inch SATA form factors. Compare PCIe generation, capacity, sustained speed and endurance for builds.
components/storage-2/ssd/internal.seo_desc_en: Internal Solid State Drives for desktop, notebook, workstation and server installations. The selection covers M.2 Non-Volatile Memory Express (NVMe) drives in PCIe 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0, plus 2.5-inch SATA SSDs for upgrade and notebook use. Listings show form factor, interface generation, capacity, sustained speed and Total Bytes Written endurance so the drive is matched to the workload.
components/storage-2/ssd/internal.bottom_seo_en:
Category overview

Internal SSDs are the system and primary working drives in current builds. The form factor, the interface generation and the controller design decide both the rated speed and how the drive holds up to heavy work over time.

What this category covers

M.2 2280 NVMe drives, the most common internal SSD, in PCIe 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0 generations; 2.5-inch SATA SSDs for upgrade paths and notebooks without M.2 slots; and U.2 NVMe drives for server and workstation backplanes.

How to choose an internal SSD

Confirm the form factor and interface

The motherboard manual lists the M.2 slot count, the supported lengths (usually 2280, with some boards supporting 22110 for U.2-style 22 mm by 110 mm drives), and the PCIe generation per slot. A PCIe 5.0 drive in a PCIe 4.0 slot runs at PCIe 4.0 speed but works fine; the inverse also works.

Match capacity to the role
